refashioned: Let us make a fresh start and identify the three kinds of moral states to be avoided: vice, incontinence (weakness of will), and brutishness. The opposites of two of these are clear -- we call one virtue, the other continence (self-control). As for brutishness, its most fitting opposite would be a superhuman kind of virtue -- something heroic and divine. This is the sort of excellence Homer has Priam attribute to Hector, saying that he was extraordinarily good.
elegant: There are three moral conditions to avoid: vice, weakness of will, and brutishness. Vice is opposed by virtue, weakness of will by self-control. Brutishness is best opposed by superhuman excellence -- a heroic, divine kind of virtue, such as Homer attributes to Hector through Priam's words.
summary: Aristotle identifies three negative moral states -- vice, incontinence (weakness of will), and brutishness -- and their corresponding positive opposites: virtue, continence (self-control), and a superhuman or divine kind of virtue.
distilled: The three moral conditions to avoid are vice, weakness of will, and brutishness, each opposed by virtue, self-control, and superhuman excellence respectively.
notes: Homer's Iliad (24.258-259) contains Priam's praise of Hector -- Priam is the king of Troy, Hector his greatest warrior son. -- This opening chapter sets up the topics for Book VII: incontinence, continence, and pleasure, which were deferred from the discussion of individual virtues in Books III-V. -- The notion of 'heroic virtue' beyond ordinary human excellence connects to Aristotle's concept of the magnanimous person (Book IV) and anticipates his account of contemplative happiness in Book X.

refashioned:
"For he seemed not the child of a mortal man, but as one born of divine seed."

So if, as people say, humans become gods through an extraordinary excess of virtue, this godlike state must be what stands opposite to the brutish state. For just as a brute possesses neither vice nor virtue, neither does a god -- the god's state is something higher than virtue, while the brute's state is something different in kind from vice.
elegant: If extraordinary virtue makes humans godlike, then divine excellence opposes brutishness. A god transcends virtue just as a brute falls outside the category of vice -- each stands beyond the human moral spectrum in opposite directions.
summary: Divine excellence and brutishness are both beyond the normal human moral range. Gods transcend virtue entirely, while brutes fall below the threshold of vice -- neither operates within the moral categories that apply to human beings.
distilled: Gods are above virtue and brutes are below vice -- both lie outside the human moral spectrum in opposite directions.
notes: The quoted verse is from Homer's Iliad (24.258-259), where Priam laments Hector's death. -- The idea that humans can become godlike through virtue reflects a traditional Greek aspiration (theosis), though Aristotle treats it as largely theoretical given human limitations. -- This framework of god-human-brute establishes a moral hierarchy that Aristotle uses throughout the Ethics, particularly when discussing contemplative life in Book X.10.

refashioned:
Now since godlike people are rarely found -- the Spartans use the epithet 'godlike man' when they particularly admire someone -- so too the brutish type is rare among humans. It appears mainly among non-Greeks, though some brutish traits also result from disease or physical deformity. We also apply this condemning label to those who exceed all ordinary standards of vice. We will discuss this brutish disposition later; vice has already been treated above. What we must now examine is incontinence (weakness of will) and softness, together with their opposites, continence (self-control) and endurance. We should treat neither pair as identical with virtue or wickedness, nor as belonging to a completely different category.

We must follow our usual method: lay out the observed facts, first work through the difficulties, and then establish the truth of all the common opinions about these states of mind -- or, failing that, of the majority and the most authoritative ones. For if we can both resolve the objections and leave the common opinions undisturbed, we will have made the case sufficiently.
elegant:
Godlike and brutish people are both rare. Brutishness appears mainly among non-Greeks, or results from disease and deformity. Having already treated vice, we must now examine weakness of will and softness alongside their opposites, self-control and endurance -- treating them as neither identical with virtue and vice nor wholly separate from them.

Our method is to lay out the observed facts, work through the difficulties, and then vindicate as many common opinions as possible. If we can resolve the objections while preserving these opinions, the case is sufficiently proved.
summary: Aristotle transitions to his main topic: incontinence and softness versus continence and endurance. He outlines his dialectical method -- start from commonly held opinions and observed facts, work through the puzzles, and try to vindicate as many of these opinions as possible.
distilled: The proper method in ethics is to begin with common opinions and observed facts, work through the difficulties they raise, and vindicate as many of those opinions as possible.
notes: The Spartans were famous for their laconic speech and reserved praise; calling someone 'godlike' was exceptional. -- 'Barbarians' (barbaroi) in Greek meant non-Greek-speakers generally, not necessarily 'uncivilized' in the modern pejorative sense. -- The dialectical method described here -- starting from endoxa (reputable opinions) and working through aporiai (puzzles) -- is Aristotle's characteristic philosophical method, described more fully in Topics I and Metaphysics III. -- The distinction between incontinence/continence and vice/virtue is crucial: the incontinent person knows the good but fails to do it, while the vicious person has a corrupted conception of the good.

refashioned:
Here are the common opinions. (1) Both continence and endurance are considered good and praiseworthy, while both incontinence and softness are considered bad and blameworthy. The continent person is thought to be one who stands by the conclusions of his reasoning, while the incontinent person is one who abandons them. (2) The incontinent person, knowing that what he does is bad, does it anyway because of passion, while the continent person, knowing that his appetites are bad, refuses to follow them because of his rational principle. (3) Everyone calls the temperate person continent and disposed to endurance, but regarding the continent person, some hold that he is always temperate while others deny this. Some call the self-indulgent person incontinent and the incontinent person self-indulgent interchangeably, while others distinguish between them. (4) People sometimes say that the person of practical wisdom cannot be incontinent, but at other times they say that some who are practically wise and clever are in fact incontinent. (5) Finally, people are said to be incontinent even with respect to anger, honor, and gain.

These, then, are the opinions that have been expressed.
elegant: Common opinions hold that: (1) continence and endurance are praiseworthy, incontinence and softness blameworthy; (2) the incontinent person knowingly does wrong from passion, while the continent person resists bad appetites through reason; (3) the temperate and continent are sometimes identified, sometimes distinguished, as are the self-indulgent and incontinent; (4) practical wisdom is sometimes said to exclude incontinence, sometimes not; (5) incontinence extends to anger, honor, and gain.
summary: Aristotle catalogues the common opinions about continence and incontinence that will generate the puzzles he needs to resolve. These include questions about the relationship between continence and temperance, whether practical wisdom prevents incontinence, and what objects incontinence concerns.
distilled: The common opinions about self-control and weakness of will generate a series of tensions that philosophical inquiry must resolve.
notes: This catalogue of endoxa (reputable opinions) follows the dialectical method outlined in 7.3. -- The distinction between temperance (sophrosyne) and continence (enkrateia) is central to Aristotle's moral psychology: the temperate person has well-ordered desires and feels no internal conflict, while the continent person experiences bad desires but overcomes them. -- The question about practical wisdom (phronesis) and incontinence connects to Book VI's account of phronesis. -- Opinion (5) about incontinence regarding anger, honor, and gain will be addressed in chapters 4-6.

refashioned:
We may now raise the following puzzles. (1) How can someone who judges correctly behave incontinently? Some say it is impossible when the person truly has knowledge. Socrates thought it would be absurd if, when knowledge was present in a person, something else could overpower it and drag it around like a slave. Socrates entirely rejected the existence of incontinence: no one, he maintained, acts against what he judges to be best -- people only act badly through ignorance.

This view plainly contradicts the observed facts, and we must investigate what actually happens to the incontinent person. If he acts from ignorance, what kind of ignorance is involved? For it is clear that the person who behaves incontinently did not, before falling into this state, think he ought to act that way.

Some thinkers accept certain parts of Socrates' position but not others. They grant that nothing is stronger than knowledge, but deny that no one acts against what has seemed to him the better course. They therefore say that the incontinent person, when mastered by pleasure, has mere opinion rather than knowledge. But if it is only opinion and not knowledge -- if the conviction resisting appetite is weak rather than strong, as with people who hesitate -- we would sympathize with their failure to stand firm. Yet we do not sympathize with wickedness or any other blameworthy state.

Is it then practical wisdom whose resistance is overcome? That is the strongest of all cognitive states. But this leads to absurdity: the same person would be both practically wise and incontinent, and no one would say that it belongs to a practically wise person to willingly do the worst acts. Moreover, it has been shown earlier that the practically wise person is one who acts (since practical wisdom deals with particular facts) and who possesses the other virtues as well.
elegant: Socrates denied incontinence entirely: no one knowingly acts against their best judgment -- they only err through ignorance. This contradicts the observed facts. Some thinkers compromise by saying the incontinent person has mere opinion, not knowledge. But if the conviction is weak, we would sympathize with failure to resist -- yet we blame incontinence. If instead it is practical wisdom being overcome, that is absurd, since practical wisdom is the strongest cognitive state and essentially includes right action and the other virtues.
summary: Aristotle examines Socrates' view that incontinence is impossible because no one knowingly acts against their best judgment. While this contradicts observed experience, attempts to save part of Socrates' position -- by distinguishing knowledge from opinion, or by identifying the overcome state as practical wisdom -- each generate their own difficulties.
distilled: Socrates' denial that anyone knowingly acts against their better judgment contradicts experience, yet attempts to explain incontinence by weakening the cognitive state involved lead to further problems.
notes: Socrates' intellectualist position that 'no one does wrong willingly' appears in Plato's Protagoras (352a-358d), where Socrates argues that apparent weakness of will is really ignorance of the good. -- This is one of the most famous passages in ancient moral psychology, raising what is now called the problem of akrasia (weakness of will). -- The reference to practical wisdom (phronesis) connects to Book VI, especially VI.12-13, where Aristotle argues that phronesis requires all the virtues. -- Aristotle's resolution (in 7.3/chapter 3) will involve distinguishing different senses of 'knowing,' partially vindicating Socrates.

refashioned: (2) Furthermore, if continence involves having strong and bad appetites, then the temperate person will not be continent, nor the continent person temperate. A temperate person has neither excessive nor bad appetites. But the continent person must have them: if the appetites being restrained were good, the state of character that holds us back from following them would be bad, so not all continence would be good. On the other hand, if the appetites are weak and not bad, there is nothing admirable in resisting them; and if they are weak and bad, there is nothing impressive about resisting those either.
elegant: If continence requires strong, bad appetites, then the temperate person (who has neither excessive nor bad appetites) cannot be continent. But if the appetites being restrained were good, restraining them would be bad. And if the appetites are weak, resisting them is neither admirable nor impressive.
summary: A puzzle arises about the relationship between temperance and continence. If continence requires resisting strong bad desires, the temperate person (who lacks such desires) cannot be continent. But this seems to separate two states that common opinion often identifies.
distilled: Continence and temperance cannot be identical because continence presupposes the bad desires that temperance has already eliminated.
notes: This puzzle sharpens the distinction between temperance (sophrosyne) and continence (enkrateia) introduced in 7.4. -- Temperance was discussed in Book III.10-12, where the temperate person is characterized as having well-ordered desires rather than needing to struggle against bad ones. -- This distinction became foundational in later moral philosophy, especially in Kant's contrast between acting from duty against inclination and acting from a well-formed character.

refashioned: (3) Furthermore, if continence makes a person ready to stand by any and every opinion, it could be bad -- for instance, if it makes someone cling to a false opinion. And if incontinence makes a person liable to abandon any and every opinion, there could be a praiseworthy form of incontinence. Sophocles' Neoptolemus in the Philoctetes provides an example: he deserves praise for not standing by what Odysseus had persuaded him to do, because he was pained at having to tell a lie.
elegant: If continence means standing by every opinion, it could be bad -- it might mean clinging to falsehood. If incontinence means abandoning any opinion, it could be good -- as when Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes rightly abandoned the deception Odysseus had urged upon him, because lying caused him pain.
summary: If continence simply means standing firm on any belief, it could be a fault (stubbornness in error). If incontinence simply means abandoning beliefs, it could be praiseworthy -- as when Neoptolemus abandoned a lie he had been persuaded to tell.
distilled: Standing firm is not always good and giving way is not always bad -- what matters is whether the conviction being held or abandoned is right.
notes: Neoptolemus is the son of Achilles in Sophocles' Philoctetes (produced 409 BCE). Odysseus persuades him to deceive the wounded Philoctetes into giving up Heracles' bow, but Neoptolemus cannot bear the dishonesty and confesses the plot. -- This example recurs at 7.9/7.38 and is resolved there. -- The puzzle highlights that continence cannot be defined merely as 'standing by one's convictions' without reference to the truth or rightness of those convictions.

refashioned:
(4) Furthermore, the sophistical argument creates a puzzle. This is the kind of syllogism that aims to produce paradoxical conclusions from an opponent's premises, so that the sophist gains admiration when he succeeds. The mind gets trapped -- unable to rest content with the conclusion because it is dissatisfied, yet unable to advance because it cannot refute the reasoning.

From this kind of argument it follows that foolishness combined with incontinence is virtue. For if a foolish person judges what is good to be evil and something he should not do, and then, through incontinence, does the opposite of what he judges, he will end up doing what is good rather than what is evil.
elegant: Sophistical reasoning produces a paradox: if a fool judges the good to be evil and something to avoid, and then through weakness of will does the opposite of his judgment, he ends up doing what is actually good. This would make foolishness combined with incontinence a kind of virtue -- an absurd conclusion.
summary: A sophistical paradox: if a foolish person misjudges good as evil, and then through weakness of will acts against his own (wrong) judgment, he accidentally does the right thing. This would absurdly make folly plus incontinence equivalent to virtue.
distilled: The sophistic paradox that a fool's weakness of will could accidentally produce right action shows that incontinence cannot be defined without reference to correct judgment.
notes: Sophists were itinerant teachers of rhetoric and argument in 5th-4th century BCE Greece; Aristotle frequently criticizes their methods in the Sophistical Refutations and Rhetoric. -- This puzzle anticipates Aristotle's resolution that incontinence properly so-called involves acting against correct judgment, not just against any judgment. -- The logical structure resembles the 'two wrongs make a right' fallacy.

refashioned:
(5) Furthermore, someone who pursues and chooses what is pleasant based on settled conviction would seem to be better off than someone who does so through incontinence rather than rational calculation, since the former is easier to cure -- he might be persuaded to change his mind. But the incontinent person invites the proverb: 'When water chokes you, what can you wash it down with?' If he had been convinced that what he was doing was right, he would stop when persuaded otherwise. But as things stand, he acts despite already being convinced of the opposite.

(6) Furthermore, if incontinence and continence can concern any and every kind of object, who qualifies as incontinent without qualification? No one has every form of incontinence, yet we do call some people incontinent in an unqualified sense.
elegant: Someone who pursues pleasure from conviction is easier to cure than the incontinent person, who already believes otherwise yet acts badly anyway -- prompting the proverb 'When water chokes you, what can you wash it down with?' Additionally, if incontinence could concern anything at all, there would be no unqualified sense of the term, yet we do speak of people as simply 'incontinent.'
summary: Two further puzzles: first, the self-indulgent person who acts from conviction seems more curable than the incontinent person who already knows better but acts wrongly anyway. Second, if incontinence could apply to any object whatsoever, there would be no way to identify someone as incontinent in the unqualified sense -- yet we clearly do make such judgments.
distilled: The incontinent person presents a deeper puzzle than the self-indulgent one: he already holds the right conviction but acts against it, and his incontinence must have a determinate scope if the concept is to be meaningful.
notes: The proverb 'when water chokes, what is one to wash it down with?' is a Greek expression for being undermined by the very thing that should help -- comparable to 'physician, heal thyself.' -- Puzzle (5) sets up the important distinction between self-indulgence (akolasia) and incontinence (akrasia) that Aristotle resolves in chapter 8: the self-indulgent person is actually worse because his first principles are corrupted, even though the incontinent person's condition seems more paradoxical. -- Puzzle (6) is resolved in chapter 4, where Aristotle limits unqualified incontinence to bodily pleasures.

refashioned:
Such are the kinds of difficulties that arise. Some of these points must be refuted and others left standing, for solving these difficulties is itself the discovery of the truth.

We must consider first (1) whether incontinent people act knowingly or not, and in what sense 'knowingly'; then (2) what sorts of objects the incontinent and continent person are concerned with -- whether any and every pleasure and pain, or only certain determinate kinds -- and whether the continent person and the person of endurance are the same or different. We must address the other questions relevant to this inquiry as well.

Our starting point is (a) the question whether the continent and incontinent person are distinguished by their objects or by their attitude -- that is, whether someone is incontinent simply by being concerned with certain objects, or rather by relating to them in a certain way, or by both. The second question (b) is whether incontinence and continence concern any and every object or not.

The person who is incontinent in the unqualified sense is not concerned with just any object, but precisely with those same objects that concern the self-indulgent person. Nor is he characterized simply by his relation to these objects (for that would make his state identical to self-indulgence), but by a specific kind of relation. The self-indulgent person is led on by deliberate choice, believing that he should always pursue the present pleasure. The incontinent person does not believe this, yet pursues the pleasure all the same.
elegant:
Solving these puzzles is itself the discovery of truth. We must determine: (1) in what sense the incontinent person acts 'knowingly,' and (2) what objects incontinence concerns.

The incontinent person in the unqualified sense deals with the same objects as the self-indulgent person -- bodily pleasures -- but relates to them differently. The self-indulgent person deliberately chooses to pursue present pleasure, believing he should. The incontinent person does not believe he should, yet pursues it anyway.
summary: Aristotle begins resolving the puzzles. Unqualified incontinence concerns the same bodily pleasures as self-indulgence but differs in attitude: the self-indulgent person deliberately chooses excess pleasure believing it right, while the incontinent person pursues it despite believing otherwise.
distilled: What distinguishes incontinence from self-indulgence is not the object but the attitude: the incontinent person acts against his own better judgment, while the self-indulgent person acts in accordance with his corrupted judgment.
notes: This chapter begins Aristotle's positive resolution of the puzzles raised in chapters 1-2. -- The distinction between incontinence and self-indulgence rests on the role of choice (prohairesis), one of Aristotle's key ethical concepts defined in Book III.2-3. The self-indulgent person's choice is corrupted; the incontinent person's choice remains correct but is overridden by appetite. -- The method of resolving aporiai (puzzles) to discover truth is characteristic of Aristotelian dialectic, described in Topics I and Metaphysics III.

refashioned:
(1) As for the suggestion that what the incontinent person acts against is true opinion rather than knowledge, this makes no difference to the argument. Some people in a state of opinion do not hesitate at all but believe they know with certainty. If the claim is that those with mere opinion, having weaker conviction, are more likely to act against their judgment than those with knowledge, we must reply that there need be no difference between knowledge and opinion in this respect. Some people are no less convinced of what they believe than others are of what they know -- as the case of Heraclitus demonstrates.

But (a) since we use the word 'know' in two senses -- both the person who possesses knowledge but is not currently using it and the person who is actively using it are said to 'know' -- it will make a difference whether the person who does what he should not possesses the knowledge without exercising it, or is actively exercising it. The latter case seems genuinely strange, but the former does not.
elegant: The distinction between opinion and knowledge does not resolve the problem of incontinence, since some people hold their opinions with conviction as strong as knowledge -- as Heraclitus illustrates. What matters is the distinction between possessing knowledge and actively using it. Acting against knowledge one possesses but is not exercising is unsurprising; acting against knowledge one is actively employing would be truly strange.
summary: Aristotle argues that the opinion-versus-knowledge distinction does not solve the puzzle of incontinence, since conviction can be equally strong in either case. The crucial distinction is instead between possessing knowledge passively and actively exercising it.
distilled: What matters for incontinence is not whether one has knowledge or opinion, but whether one's knowledge is actively engaged or merely latent.
notes: Heraclitus of Ephesus (fl. c. 500 BCE) was a pre-Socratic philosopher known for his obscure but deeply held doctrines and his supreme confidence in them despite their paradoxical nature. -- The distinction between possessing knowledge (hexis) and actively using it (energeia) is fundamental to Aristotle's philosophy, appearing in De Anima II.1, Metaphysics IX, and throughout the Ethics. -- This begins Aristotle's multi-layered analysis of how someone can 'know' yet fail to act on that knowledge, partially vindicating Socrates.

refashioned:
(b) Furthermore, since there are two kinds of premises in a syllogism, nothing prevents someone from having both premises yet acting against his knowledge, provided he is using only the universal premise and not the particular one. After all, it is particular actions that must be performed.

There are also two kinds of universal term: one refers to the agent, the other to the object. For example, the universal premise might be 'dry food is good for every person,' and the agent-related premise 'I am a person,' or the object-related premise 'such-and-such food is dry.' But whether 'this particular food is of that kind' -- this is the particular premise that the incontinent person either does not have or is not actively employing.

There will therefore be an enormous difference between these ways of knowing. To have knowledge in the one way (possessing only the universal) when acting incontinently would not seem at all strange, but to have it in the other way (grasping the particular conclusion as well) would be extraordinary.
elegant: A practical syllogism has universal and particular premises. One can hold the universal premise ('dry food benefits everyone') without grasping or applying the particular ('this food is dry'). Since action depends on the particular premise, someone can possess knowledge in the universal sense while failing to apply it in the particular case. This explains how one can 'know' yet act incontinently without paradox.
summary: Aristotle introduces the practical syllogism to explain incontinence. Someone may hold the universal premise ('such food is good for everyone') without grasping or activating the relevant particular premise ('this food before me is of that kind'). Since action requires the particular premise, one can know in general yet fail to act rightly in the specific case.
distilled: One can possess universal knowledge about what is good yet fail to recognize or apply it in the particular case at hand, because action depends on grasping the particular premise.
notes: The practical syllogism is one of Aristotle's most important contributions to moral psychology and action theory. It appears also in De Motu Animalium 7 and De Anima III.11. -- The example 'dry food is good for every man' reflects ancient Greek medical theory (Hippocratic medicine classified foods by qualities like dry/moist, hot/cold). -- This analysis shows how Aristotle partially vindicates Socrates: the incontinent person does lack a certain kind of knowledge -- not universal knowledge, but active particular knowledge.

refashioned:
(c) Furthermore, there is yet another way of possessing knowledge without using it. Within the category of having knowledge but not employing it, we can distinguish different states -- including the possibility of having knowledge in a sense while not having it, as with someone asleep, insane, or drunk. But this is precisely the condition of people under the influence of strong passions. Outbursts of anger, sexual desire, and other such passions clearly alter our very bodily condition, and in some people even produce fits of madness. It is plain, then, that incontinent people must be said to be in a condition similar to those who are asleep, insane, or drunk.

The fact that such people use language that sounds knowledgeable proves nothing. Even people under the influence of these passions can recite scientific proofs and quote Empedocles, and those who have just begun learning a subject can string together its terminology without truly knowing it -- for knowledge must become part of oneself, and that takes time. We must therefore suppose that the words of people in an incontinent state mean no more than lines spoken by actors on stage.

(d) We can also analyze the cause by looking at how human cognition actually works. One kind of judgment is universal; the other concerns particular facts and falls within the domain of perception. When a single conclusion results from combining these two, the mind must, in theoretical matters, affirm the conclusion, and in practical matters, act on it immediately. For example, if one holds that 'everything sweet ought to be tasted' and perceives that 'this is sweet' (as one of the particular sweet things), the person who is able to act and is not prevented must at the same time actually taste it.

Now when a universal judgment is present in us telling us to avoid something, and simultaneously the judgment that 'everything sweet is pleasant' is present along with the perception that 'this particular thing is sweet' (and it is this last judgment that is active), and when appetite also happens to be present -- then the universal judgment tells us to avoid the object, but appetite drives us toward it (for appetite can move each of our bodily parts). And so it turns out that a person behaves incontinently under the influence, in a sense, of a rational principle and an opinion -- one that is not contrary in itself to the right principle, but only incidentally so. For it is the appetite that is contrary to right reason, not the opinion.

This also explains why the lower animals are not incontinent: they have no universal judgments, only imagination and memory of particulars.
elegant:
People gripped by passion are like those asleep, mad, or drunk -- they may possess knowledge without being able to use it. Their recitation of correct principles proves nothing, just as actors speak lines without understanding them.

The mechanism works thus: a universal judgment ('avoid this') coexists with another practical premise ('everything sweet is pleasant') and a perception ('this is sweet'). Appetite, activated by the particular perception, drives the person toward the object even while the universal judgment forbids it. The person acts incontinently under the influence of a kind of reasoning -- but it is appetite, not the opinion itself, that opposes right reason.

This is why animals cannot be incontinent: lacking universal judgments, they have nothing to act against.
summary: Aristotle offers his central explanation of incontinence. People in the grip of passion are like the sleeping or drunk -- they possess knowledge but cannot activate it. The mechanism involves competing practical syllogisms: a universal prohibition and a particular appetite-driven premise. Appetite moves the person to act on the particular perception while the universal knowledge is temporarily disabled. Animals cannot be incontinent because they lack universal judgments entirely.
distilled: Passion temporarily disables one's grasp of universal knowledge, allowing appetite -- working through a particular perception of pleasure -- to drive action against what one knows in general to be right.
notes: Empedocles of Acragas (c. 494-434 BCE) was a pre-Socratic philosopher, poet, and wonder-worker whose verses were widely memorized. -- The comparison to actors (hypokritai) is striking: the Greek word for actor literally means 'answerer' or 'interpreter.' -- The practical syllogism framework here is Aristotle's most developed account, involving competing syllogisms with shared particular premises. -- The exclusion of animals from incontinence because they lack universal judgment connects to De Anima III.11 and Aristotle's broader theory of animal cognition. -- This passage is central to the entire history of weakness of will (akrasia) in philosophy, influencing Donald Davidson's 'How is Weakness of the Will Possible?' (1970) and much subsequent debate.

refashioned:
The explanation of how the ignorance dissolves and the incontinent person regains his knowledge is the same as for someone who is drunk or asleep, and is not unique to this condition -- we must consult the natural scientists for the details.

Now, the final premise -- which is both a judgment about a perceptible object and the one that determines action -- is something the person in a state of passion either does not possess at all, or possesses only in the sense that 'having knowledge' means merely being able to utter words, as a drunk person might recite Empedocles. And because this last term is not universal and not an object of scientific knowledge in the way the universal term is, the position Socrates was trying to establish actually turns out to be correct. For incontinence does not arise in the presence of what is properly called knowledge, nor is it genuine knowledge that gets 'dragged about' by passion. Rather, it occurs in the presence of perceptual knowledge only.

This must suffice as our answer to the question of how it is possible to behave incontinently while possessing knowledge, and in what sense the incontinent person does and does not 'know.'
elegant:
When passion subsides, the incontinent person recovers his knowledge, just as the drunk or sleeping person does. The crucial point is that incontinence occurs not in the presence of genuine scientific knowledge but only when perceptual knowledge of particulars is involved. Thus Socrates was essentially right: it is not knowledge in the proper sense that gets overpowered by passion, but only perception-level cognition.

This resolves how one can behave incontinently while in some sense 'knowing.'
summary: Aristotle concludes his analysis of knowledge and incontinence. The incontinent person's universal knowledge remains intact; what passion overwhelms is only the particular, perception-level judgment that determines action. Socrates was therefore substantially correct: genuine knowledge in the full sense is not what gets overpowered.
distilled: Socrates was essentially right that knowledge proper is not overcome by passion -- what incontinence overwhelms is only the particular, perceptual judgment needed to trigger action.
notes: This passage represents Aristotle's characteristic method of partially vindicating his predecessors while correcting them. Socrates was right about knowledge-proper but wrong to deny incontinence entirely. -- Empedocles is mentioned again as the example of someone whose words can be mechanically repeated. -- The appeal to 'students of natural science' (physikoi) for the mechanism of recovery reflects Aristotle's view that ethics and natural philosophy are complementary disciplines. -- This resolution has been enormously influential but also contested; many scholars debate whether Aristotle truly solves the problem or merely redescribes it.

refashioned:
(2) We must next consider whether anyone is incontinent without qualification, or whether everyone who is incontinent is so only in some particular respect. And if there is incontinence without qualification, we must determine what sorts of objects it concerns.

It is evident that both continent and enduring persons, and both incontinent and soft persons, are concerned with pleasures and pains.
elegant: We must determine whether incontinence exists in an unqualified sense or only in qualified forms. Both continent and incontinent persons, and both enduring and soft persons, clearly deal with pleasures and pains.
summary: Aristotle transitions to the question of whether there is incontinence in an unqualified sense or only qualified forms, establishing that all these dispositions -- continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness -- concern pleasures and pains.
distilled: The question is whether incontinence has an unqualified form or exists only in qualified varieties, given that all such dispositions concern pleasures and pains.
notes: This opens the discussion of chapters 4-6 (or 7.15-7.27 in the thought numbering), which address puzzle (6) from chapter 2 about what objects incontinence properly concerns. -- The pairing of continence/incontinence with endurance/softness reflects the dual axes of pleasure-resistance and pain-resistance. -- 'Without qualification' (haplos) versus 'in a particular respect' (kata ti) is a standard Aristotelian distinction used across his works.

refashioned:
Among the things that produce pleasure, some are necessary while others are worthy of choice in themselves but admit of excess. The bodily sources of pleasure are necessary -- by which I mean those related to food and sexual intercourse, the very bodily matters we defined as the concern of self-indulgence and temperance. The others are not necessary but are worthy of choice in themselves -- for example, victory, honor, wealth, and similar good and pleasant things.

This being so, (a) those who go to excess regarding the latter category, contrary to the right principle within them, are not called incontinent without qualification, but incontinent 'with respect to money,' 'gain,' 'honor,' or 'anger.' They are not called simply incontinent because they are different from incontinent people proper and are called incontinent only by resemblance. (Compare the case of Anthropos -- whose name means 'Man' -- who won a contest at the Olympic games: the general definition of 'man' differed only slightly from the definition specific to him, but it was different nonetheless.) This distinction is confirmed by the fact that incontinence regarding bodily pleasure -- whether unqualified or concerning some particular bodily pleasure -- is blamed not only as a fault but as a kind of vice, while none of those who are incontinent regarding the other objects receive this kind of blame.
elegant: Pleasures divide into the necessary (food, sex -- the domain of temperance) and the non-necessary but choiceworthy (honor, wealth, victory). Excess regarding the latter is called incontinence only by analogy -- 'incontinent with respect to honor' or 'gain.' Only incontinence regarding bodily pleasure is blamed as a genuine vice, confirming that unqualified incontinence concerns bodily pleasures alone.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes necessary bodily pleasures (food, sex) from non-necessary but choiceworthy ones (honor, wealth, victory). Only excess regarding the former constitutes incontinence in the unqualified sense; excess regarding the latter is called incontinence only by analogy and is not blamed as a vice.
distilled: Incontinence without qualification concerns only necessary bodily pleasures; when we speak of incontinence regarding honor, wealth, or anger, we use the term by analogy, not in its primary sense.
notes: Anthropos ('Man') is an otherwise unknown Olympic victor whose personal name happened to be the Greek word for 'human being.' Aristotle uses this as a witty illustration of homonymy -- the same word with different definitions. -- The Olympic games were the most prestigious of the four Panhellenic athletic festivals. -- The restriction of unqualified incontinence to bodily pleasures connects back to the account of temperance in Book III.10-12, where Aristotle similarly restricts temperance to pleasures of touch and taste.

refashioned:
(b) Among those who are incontinent regarding bodily enjoyments -- the domain of the temperate and self-indulgent person -- the one who pursues excesses of pleasant things and avoids painful things like hunger, thirst, heat, cold, and all objects of touch and taste, doing so not by deliberate choice but contrary to his choice and judgment, is called incontinent without any qualification -- not 'incontinent with respect to this or that' (such as anger), but simply incontinent.

This is confirmed by the fact that people are called 'soft' regarding these bodily pleasures but not regarding any others. And this is why we group together the incontinent and the self-indulgent, and the continent and the temperate -- but not the other types mentioned above -- because they are all concerned in some way with the same pleasures and pains. However, though they deal with the same objects, they do not relate to them in the same way: some act from deliberate choice while others do not.
elegant: The person who pursues bodily excess and avoids bodily pain not by choice but against his own judgment is called incontinent without qualification. We group the incontinent with the self-indulgent, and the continent with the temperate, because they share the same objects -- bodily pleasures and pains. The difference is that some act from deliberate choice while others do not.
summary: Unqualified incontinence involves pursuing bodily pleasures and avoiding bodily pains against one's own better judgment. The incontinent and self-indulgent share the same objects (bodily pleasures) but differ in that the self-indulgent acts by choice while the incontinent acts against his choice.
distilled: The truly incontinent person pursues bodily pleasure against his own judgment, whereas the self-indulgent person pursues it in accordance with his -- same objects, opposite relationship to choice.
notes: The term 'soft' (malakos) for inability to withstand bodily pain confirms that softness is the pain-side counterpart to incontinence on the pleasure side. -- The role of choice (prohairesis) as the distinguishing factor between incontinence and self-indulgence is one of Aristotle's most important contributions to moral psychology. -- This passage resolves puzzle (3) from 7.4 about whether the incontinent and self-indulgent are the same: they share objects but differ in their relationship to deliberate choice.

refashioned: This is why we should call a person self-indulgent if he pursues excessive pleasures and avoids moderate pains with little or no appetite driving him, rather than one who does so because of overwhelming appetites. For consider: what would the former do if he also had a powerful appetite and felt intense pain at lacking the necessities?
elegant: The truly self-indulgent person is one who pursues excess with little or no appetite urging him on. If someone with no desire still chases excessive pleasure, imagine what he would do with powerful appetites added.
summary: True self-indulgence is revealed not by the strength of one's appetites but by pursuing excess even without strong desire. Someone who chases pleasure without being driven by appetite is worse than someone overwhelmed by powerful urges.
distilled: Self-indulgence is more clearly shown when someone pursues excess without strong appetite than when someone is overwhelmed by powerful desires.
notes: This distinction reinforces why self-indulgence is worse than incontinence: the self-indulgent person does not even need the excuse of overwhelming passion. -- The argument parallels Aristotle's general principle (stated in Book III) that actions done from choice reveal character more than actions done under compulsion or passion. -- The 'necessary objects' refers to the basic bodily needs (food, drink, warmth) discussed in Book III.11.

refashioned:
Now among appetites and pleasures, some belong to the class of things that are inherently noble and good -- for some pleasant things are by nature worthy of choice, while others are contrary to these, and still others are intermediate (adopting our earlier distinction). Examples include wealth, gain, victory, and honor.

Regarding all such objects, whether noble or intermediate, people are not blamed for being attracted to them, for desiring and loving them, but only for doing so to excess. (This is why those who, contrary to right reason, are mastered by or pursue things that are naturally noble and good -- for instance, those who concern themselves more than they should with honor, or with their children and parents -- are not wicked. These things are genuinely good, and those who care about them are praised. Yet even here excess is possible: one might fight against the gods themselves like Niobe, or be as excessively devoted to one's father as Satyrus, nicknamed 'the filial,' who was considered quite foolish on this point.)

There is no wickedness regarding these objects, for the reason given: each is by nature worthy of choice for its own sake. Yet excesses regarding them are bad and should be avoided. Similarly, there is no incontinence regarding them in the proper sense, since incontinence is not merely something to avoid but also something worthy of blame. However, because the feelings involved resemble those of genuine incontinence, people apply the term with a qualifier -- 'incontinent with respect to anger' or 'with respect to honor' -- just as we might call someone a bad doctor or a bad actor without meaning to call them simply 'bad.' Just as in that case we do not use the term 'bad' without qualification (because the condition is not badness but only analogous to it), so too only what concerns the same objects as temperance and self-indulgence should be taken as incontinence and continence in the proper sense. We apply the term to anger by virtue of resemblance, which is why we add the qualifier: 'incontinent with respect to anger,' as we say 'incontinent with respect to honor or gain.'
elegant: Some pleasures involve naturally noble objects -- honor, wealth, victory, devotion to family. Pursuing these is not blameworthy; only excess is. Since these objects are inherently good, excess regarding them is not wickedness, and failing to resist them is not incontinence proper but only incontinence by analogy. True incontinence concerns the same bodily pleasures as temperance and self-indulgence. When we speak of incontinence 'with respect to anger' or 'honor,' we use the term metaphorically, just as calling someone a 'bad doctor' does not mean they are bad without qualification.
summary: Appetites for naturally noble things like honor, family, and achievement are not blamed in themselves -- only their excess is. Because these objects are inherently good, failing to resist them is incontinence only by analogy, not in the proper sense. True incontinence is restricted to the bodily pleasures that also concern temperance and self-indulgence.
distilled: Excess in pursuing genuinely noble things like honor or family is a fault by analogy only -- true incontinence, like true temperance, concerns bodily pleasures alone.
notes: Niobe, in Greek mythology, boasted of having more children than the goddess Leto and was punished when Apollo and Artemis killed all her children; she represents the extreme of parental pride. -- Satyrus 'the filial' (philopatōr) is otherwise unknown; he serves as an example of excessive but non-vicious devotion. -- The analogy of calling someone a 'bad doctor' illustrates Aristotle's theory of homonymy (things sharing a name but not a definition), central to his logic and metaphysics. -- This passage completes the resolution of puzzle (6) from 7.4 about whether incontinence concerns any and every object.

refashioned:
(1) Some things are pleasant by nature: (a) some of these are pleasant without qualification, while (b) others are pleasant only relative to particular kinds of animals or humans. (2) Other things are not naturally pleasant, but become so: (a) some through physical injuries, (b) others through acquired habits, and (c) others because of originally defective natures.

Corresponding to each of these unnatural categories, we can identify character states analogous to those recognized for natural pleasures. I mean (A) the brutish states -- such as the woman who is said to rip open pregnant women and devour the infants, or the pleasures attributed to certain tribes around the Black Sea who have gone savage (delighting in raw meat, human flesh, or lending their children to one another to feast upon), or the stories told about Phalaris.
elegant: Some things are naturally pleasant (universally or for specific kinds), while others become pleasant through injury, habit, or defective nature. For each unnatural category there exist corresponding character states -- the brutish, as seen in reports of cannibalism, savage practices among Black Sea tribes, and the cruelties attributed to Phalaris.
summary: Aristotle classifies pleasures into the naturally pleasant (universally or for specific kinds) and the unnaturally pleasant (through injury, habit, or defective nature). Corresponding to the unnatural pleasures are brutish character states, illustrated by extreme examples of cannibalism and savagery.
distilled: Pleasures divide into the naturally pleasant and the unnaturally pleasant, with the latter giving rise to brutish states of character that fall outside the normal moral categories.
notes: Phalaris was the tyrant of Acragas (Agrigentum) in Sicily (c. 570-554 BCE), notorious for roasting victims alive inside a bronze bull. He became a byword for extreme cruelty in Greek literature. -- The Black Sea (Pontus) tribes were considered among the most remote and uncivilized peoples known to Greeks. -- This chapter (7.5 in the traditional numbering) discusses bestial and pathological states that lie outside the normal range of human vice and virtue, connecting to the framework established in 7.1-7.2.

refashioned: These states are brutish, but (B) others arise from disease or, in some cases, madness -- such as the man who sacrificed and ate his mother, or the slave who ate the liver of his fellow slave. Still others are (C) morbid conditions resulting from habit -- for example, the habit of plucking out hair, gnawing nails, or even eating coals or earth, and in addition to these, sexual abuse of children. For some of these behaviors arise from nature and in others from habit, as in those who have been victims of sexual abuse since childhood.
elegant: Beyond brutish states, some deviant pleasures arise from disease or madness (like the man who ate his mother), and others from habitual morbid conditions (hair-pulling, nail-biting, eating earth, or sexual abuse of children). These latter arise in some people by nature and in others through habit, particularly in those victimized from childhood.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes brutish states (from defective nature) from morbid states arising from disease or madness, and from habitual conditions resulting from ingrained practice. Some of these morbid habits arise from nature, others from early victimization.
distilled: Deviant pleasures arise from three distinct sources -- brutish nature, disease or madness, and ingrained morbid habit -- each requiring different classification.
notes: The specific cases mentioned (the man who sacrificed his mother, the slave who ate his companion's liver) appear to be notorious contemporary or near-contemporary incidents known to Aristotle's audience. -- Aristotle's recognition that some deviant behaviors result from childhood victimization is remarkably modern in its acknowledgment of developmental causation. -- The classification of behaviors as brutish, morbid, or habitual shows Aristotle distinguishing what we might now call psychopathology from moral vice.

refashioned:
Those in whom nature is the cause of such a state would not be called incontinent by anyone, just as we would not apply the term to women on account of their passive role in intercourse. Nor would we call those who are in a morbid condition from habit incontinent. Having these various kinds of disposition lies beyond the boundaries of vice, just as brutishness does. For someone who has these dispositions, mastering or being mastered by them is not continence or incontinence in the proper sense, but only by analogy -- just as a person in this condition regarding fits of anger should be called 'incontinent with respect to anger,' not incontinent without qualification.

Every excessive state -- whether of foolishness, cowardice, self-indulgence, or bad temper -- is either brutish or morbid. A person naturally disposed to fear everything, even the squeak of a mouse, exhibits a brutish kind of cowardice, while someone who feared a weasel did so as a consequence of disease. Among foolish people, those who are naturally thoughtless and live entirely by their senses are brutish (like some distant peoples), while those who are so because of disease such as epilepsy or madness are morbid.

It is possible to have some of these traits only at times without being mastered by them. Phalaris, for instance, may have restrained a desire to eat a child's flesh or an appetite for unnatural sexual pleasure. But it is also possible to be mastered by them, not merely to experience the urges.

Thus, just as wickedness on the human level is called wickedness simply, while that which falls outside the human level is called wickedness with the qualifier 'brutish' or 'morbid,' so too it is plain that some incontinence is brutish and some morbid, while only that which corresponds to ordinary human self-indulgence is incontinence in the proper sense.

It is clear, then, that incontinence and continence properly concern only the same objects as self-indulgence and temperance, and that what concerns other objects is a distinct type, called incontinence metaphorically rather than in the primary sense.
elegant:
Those driven by nature or disease to deviant pleasures are not incontinent in the proper sense -- their condition lies beyond vice, as brutishness does. Every extreme disposition is either brutish (from nature) or morbid (from disease). One may have such urges without being mastered by them, or one may be mastered.

Just as wickedness at the human level is wickedness simply, while brutish or morbid wickedness receives a qualifier, so incontinence properly concerns only the same objects as temperance and self-indulgence. All other applications of the term are metaphorical.
summary: Brutish and morbid conditions fall outside the normal moral categories. Incontinence and continence in the proper sense concern only the bodily pleasures that temperance and self-indulgence also address. Everything else called 'incontinence' is metaphorical -- whether it involves anger, honor, or pathological conditions.
distilled: Incontinence proper concerns only the bodily pleasures of temperance and self-indulgence; brutish and morbid conditions, like incontinence regarding anger or honor, receive the label only by metaphor.
notes: Phalaris appears again (see 7.20) -- here Aristotle makes the subtle point that even someone with brutish desires might resist them, showing that the capacity for self-control exists even outside normal moral categories. -- The distinction between brutish and morbid wickedness anticipates modern distinctions between antisocial personality traits and mental illness. -- This passage concludes the discussion of what objects incontinence properly concerns, resolving puzzle (6) from chapter 2. -- The claim that animals and distant peoples are 'brutish' reflects Greek cultural assumptions but the underlying philosophical distinction between natural disposition and moral character remains important.

refashioned:
We will now show that incontinence regarding anger is less disgraceful than incontinence regarding the appetites.

(1) Anger seems to listen to reason to some extent, but to mishear it -- like hasty servants who rush out before hearing the full message and then muddle the order, or like dogs that bark at a knock on the door without checking whether it is a friend. Similarly, anger, because of its hot and hasty nature, hears but does not properly hear the command, and springs to take revenge. For when reasoning or imagination informs us that we have been insulted or slighted, anger -- reasoning, as it were, that such treatment must be fought against -- boils up immediately. Appetite, by contrast, needs only for reasoning or perception to declare that something is pleasant, and it springs straight to enjoyment.

Therefore anger obeys reason in a sense, while appetite does not. Incontinence regarding appetite is thus more disgraceful: the person who is incontinent in anger is in a sense overcome by a (mis)application of reason, while the one who succumbs to appetite is conquered by appetite alone, not by reason at all.
elegant: Anger is less disgraceful than appetite because anger at least responds to reason, even if it mishears -- like hasty servants who garble orders, or dogs that bark before checking who knocked. Anger receives the message of insult and rashly infers 'fight back,' while appetite simply perceives 'pleasant' and springs to enjoyment. The angry person is overcome by a crude form of reasoning; the appetitive person is conquered by desire with no reasoning at all.
summary: Incontinence regarding anger is less blameworthy than incontinence regarding bodily appetite because anger at least partially responds to reason -- it hears the judgment of insult but reacts too hastily. Appetite bypasses reason entirely, springing directly to enjoyment the moment something is perceived as pleasant.
distilled: Anger is less disgraceful than appetite because anger at least engages with reason, however imperfectly, while appetite bypasses reason entirely.
notes: The vivid analogies of hasty servants and barking dogs illustrate Aristotle's view that anger has a quasi-rational structure -- it responds to perceived insults (a cognitive appraisal) but acts before fully processing the information. -- This account of anger as involving a kind of practical reasoning connects to Aristotle's Rhetoric II.2, where anger is defined as a desire for apparent revenge accompanied by pain at apparent slight. -- The chapter begins Aristotle's comparative evaluation of different forms of incontinence, arguing for a hierarchy of blameworthiness.

refashioned:
(2) Furthermore, we pardon people more readily for following natural desires, since we are more forgiving of appetites that are common to all people, and to the extent that they are universal. Now anger and bad temper are more natural than appetites for excess -- that is, for unnecessary pleasures.

Consider the man who defended himself against the charge of striking his father by saying: 'Yes, but he struck his father, and his father struck his father before him,' and then, pointing to his child, 'This boy will strike me when he is a man -- it runs in the family.' Or consider the man who, while being dragged along by his son, told him to stop at the doorway, since he himself had dragged his own father only that far.
elegant: We pardon natural desires more readily, and anger is more natural than appetite for unnecessary excess. As the anecdotes illustrate -- a man defending himself for striking his father by claiming it runs in the family, or a father being dragged by his son asking him to stop at the doorway because he had dragged his own father only that far -- anger has a natural, almost hereditary quality that elicits more sympathy than indulgence in unnecessary pleasure.
summary: Anger deserves more pardon than appetitive excess because anger is more natural and universal. Aristotle illustrates with darkly humorous anecdotes about intergenerational violence: a man who defends striking his father by calling it a family tradition, and a father who, while being dragged by his son, asks him to stop where he stopped with his own father.
distilled: Anger deserves more pardon than appetite for excess because it is more natural and universal, as illustrated by the near-instinctive, hereditary quality of aggressive impulses.
notes: These anecdotes appear to be well-known Greek jokes or legal stories. The dark humor about intergenerational violence illustrates how deeply ingrained anger is in human nature. -- The distinction between natural/necessary and unnatural/unnecessary appetites was established in Book III.11, where Aristotle distinguishes universal human desires (hunger, thirst) from individual excessive desires. -- The argument that more natural impulses are more pardonable reflects a consistent Aristotelian principle: we are less responsible for what is deeply rooted in our nature.

refashioned:
(3) Furthermore, those who are more given to plotting are more criminal. A passionate person does not scheme, and anger itself is open and unconcealed. But appetite is crafty by nature, as illustrated by what the poets call Aphrodite: 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus,' and by Homer's description of her embroidered girdle:

'And the whisper of wooing is there, whose subtlety steals the wits of the wise, however prudent they be.'

Therefore, if incontinence regarding appetite is more criminal and disgraceful than incontinence regarding anger, it is both incontinence in the unqualified sense and, in a way, a kind of vice.
elegant: Those who plot are more criminal, and appetite is inherently more cunning than anger. Anger is open; appetite is deceptive -- as the poets say of Aphrodite, 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus,' whose 'subtlety steals the wits of the wise.' Therefore incontinence regarding appetite is more criminal and more truly deserves the name incontinence.
summary: A third reason appetite-based incontinence is worse: appetite involves cunning and deception, while anger is open and straightforward. The poets describe Aphrodite (desire) as 'guile-weaving,' whose seductions steal the wits even of the wise. Plotting makes a wrongdoer more criminal.
distilled: Appetite is more culpable than anger because it operates through cunning and deception, while anger acts openly and without scheming.
notes: Aphrodite is called 'guile-weaving daughter of Cyprus' (doloplokos Kuprogenes) in a tradition going back to Sappho (fragment 1). Cyprus was one of her chief cult centers. -- The Homeric quotation is from Iliad 14.214-217, describing the magic girdle (kestos himas) that Aphrodite lends to Hera to seduce Zeus. -- The contrast between anger's openness and appetite's cunning reflects a broader Greek cultural value: frank speech (parrhesia) was admired, while deception was despised. -- This argument adds a moral dimension beyond the psychological one in 7.23.

refashioned: (4) Furthermore, no one commits wanton outrage with a feeling of pain, but everyone who acts in anger acts with pain, while the person who commits outrage acts with pleasure. If, then, the acts that most justly provoke anger are the more criminal ones, the incontinence that stems from appetite is more criminal still -- for there is no wanton outrage involved in anger.
elegant: Angry action involves pain; wanton outrage involves pleasure. Since outrageous acts done with pleasure are more criminal than those done in pain, incontinence from appetite (which involves pleasure) is worse than incontinence from anger (which involves pain).
summary: A fourth argument: anger involves pain (at perceived injustice), while appetitive indulgence involves pleasure. Since acting with pleasure in wrongdoing (wanton outrage) is more criminal than acting from pain, appetite-driven incontinence is worse than anger-driven incontinence.
distilled: Wrongdoing accompanied by pleasure is more criminal than wrongdoing accompanied by pain, making appetitive incontinence worse than angry incontinence.
notes: The term 'wanton outrage' (hubris) is a key concept in Greek ethics and law. Hubris involves deliberately humiliating someone for one's own pleasure and was a serious legal offense in Athens. -- The argument that pleasure in wrongdoing increases its gravity connects to Aristotle's discussion of voluntary action in Book III and anticipates modern retributive theories that consider the perpetrator's mental state. -- This is the fourth and final argument in Aristotle's case that incontinence regarding appetite is worse than incontinence regarding anger.

refashioned:
It is plain, then, that incontinence regarding appetite is more disgraceful than that regarding anger, and that continence and incontinence in the proper sense concern bodily appetites and pleasures.

But we must also grasp the differences among bodily pleasures themselves. As stated at the beginning, some are human and natural in both kind and degree, others are brutish, and still others result from organic injuries and diseases. Only the first kind concerns temperance and self-indulgence. This is why we do not call the lower animals temperate or self-indulgent except metaphorically -- and only when one entire species exceeds another in wantonness, destructiveness, or indiscriminate greed. Animals lack the power of choice and rational calculation; they are departures from the natural norm, as madmen are among humans.

Brutishness is a lesser evil than vice, though more alarming. It is not that the higher part of the soul has been corrupted, as happens in human vice -- brutes have no higher part to corrupt. It is like comparing a lifeless thing with a living one in respect of harmfulness: the destructiveness of that which lacks an internal source of purposive movement is always less damaging, and reason is precisely such a source. It is like comparing injustice as an abstract concept with an actual unjust person. Each is worse in some respect, but a bad human being will do ten thousand times more evil than a brute.
elegant:
Appetite-based incontinence is more disgraceful than anger-based. Among bodily pleasures themselves, only the naturally human kind concerns temperance proper -- animals lack choice and calculation, so they are called temperate or self-indulgent only metaphorically.

Brutishness, though more alarming than vice, is a lesser evil: brutes lack the higher rational faculty that vice corrupts. A lifeless thing's destructiveness is less harmful than a living thing's, because reason -- the source of purposive action -- can multiply evil enormously. An unjust person does ten thousand times more harm than a brute.
summary: Appetite-based incontinence is conclusively worse than anger-based. Among bodily pleasures, only naturally human ones concern temperance. Brutishness, though frightening, is a lesser evil than human vice because brutes lack the rational faculty whose corruption makes vice so destructive. A wicked human causes vastly more harm than any beast.
distilled: Brutishness is more alarming but less evil than human vice, because reason -- the very faculty that vice corrupts -- is what gives human wickedness its extraordinary destructive power.
notes: The claim that animals lack choice (prohairesis) and calculation (logismos) is central to Aristotle's moral psychology and his broader philosophical anthropology in De Anima and Politics I. -- The comparison of injustice-in-the-abstract with an unjust person echoes Plato's Republic (Book I) and Aristotle's own discussion in the Categories and Metaphysics of whether abstractions or their instances are more real. -- The 'ten thousand times' figure is rhetorical but makes a genuine philosophical point: rational agency amplifies both good and evil. -- This passage completes chapters 4-6's discussion and transitions to the detailed analysis of continence and incontinence in chapter 7.

refashioned:
Regarding the pleasures, pains, appetites, and aversions arising through touch and taste -- to which both self-indulgence and temperance were earlier narrowed down -- it is possible to be in a state where one is defeated even by those that most people master, or where one masters even those by which most people are defeated. Among these possibilities, those relating to pleasures are incontinence and continence; those relating to pains are softness and endurance.

The condition of most people falls in between, even if they tend to lean toward the worse states.
elegant: Within the pleasures and pains of touch and taste, one can either resist what most people resist, or be defeated by what most resist, or resist what defeats most. Regarding pleasures, these states are continence and incontinence; regarding pains, endurance and softness. Most people fall in between, leaning toward the worse side.
summary: Aristotle maps out the dispositions regarding bodily pleasure and pain. Continence and incontinence concern whether one masters or is defeated by pleasures; endurance and softness concern the same for pains. Most people occupy the middle ground, tilting slightly toward the worse states.
distilled: Continence and incontinence measure one's resistance to pleasure, while endurance and softness measure one's resistance to pain, with most people falling in the intermediate range.
notes: The restriction to touch and taste goes back to Book III.10, where Aristotle argues that temperance concerns only these two senses, not sight, hearing, or smell. -- The four-fold schema (continence/incontinence for pleasure, endurance/softness for pain) provides a systematic framework that parallels the temperance/self-indulgence pair. -- The observation that most people lean toward worse states reflects Aristotle's realism about human nature, echoing his general view that virtue is difficult and vice is easy (Book II.9).

refashioned:
Since some pleasures are necessary (up to a point, though their excesses and deficiencies are not), while others are not necessary, and the same holds for appetites and pains: the person who pursues excesses of pleasant things, or pursues necessary objects to excess, doing so by deliberate choice, for their own sake and not for the sake of any further result, is self-indulgent. Such a person is necessarily unlikely to feel regret, and is therefore incurable, since someone who cannot regret cannot be cured. The person deficient in pursuit of pleasure is the opposite of the self-indulgent; the intermediate person is temperate.

Similarly, there is the person who avoids bodily pains not because he is overpowered by them but by choice. Among those who do not choose their actions, one type is led to act by the pleasure involved, another because he is avoiding the pain of unfulfilled appetite -- so these types differ from one another. Everyone would judge more harshly a person who does something disgraceful with little or no appetite urging him on than one who acts under the influence of powerful appetite, and more harshly one who strikes without anger than one who does so in anger. For what would such a person do if he were strongly affected? This is why the self-indulgent person is worse than the incontinent.

Of the states described, then, the latter -- avoiding pain by choice -- is rather a kind of softness, while the former is self-indulgence proper. The incontinent person is opposed to the continent, and the soft to the person of endurance. Endurance consists in resisting, while continence consists in conquering, and resisting and conquering are different, just as not being beaten is different from winning. This is why continence is more worthy of choice than endurance.

The person who is defective in resisting things that most people both resist and resist successfully is soft and effeminate -- for effeminacy is a kind of softness. Such a person trails his cloak to avoid the pain of lifting it and plays the invalid without considering himself wretched, even though the person he imitates truly is wretched.
elegant:
The self-indulgent person pursues excessive pleasure by choice and without regret -- making him incurable. The temperate person is the mean; the person deficient in pleasure-pursuit is the opposite extreme.

Self-indulgence is worse than incontinence because acting disgracefully without strong appetite reveals deeper corruption. Softness is the pain-side counterpart: choosing to avoid any bodily discomfort.

Endurance means resisting pain; continence means conquering pleasure. Resisting and conquering differ as 'not being beaten' differs from 'winning' -- so continence is more choiceworthy than endurance. The soft person fails to resist what most people handle easily, trailing his cloak to avoid the effort of lifting it.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes self-indulgence (choosing excess pleasure without regret, therefore incurable) from incontinence (pursuing excess against one's judgment, therefore curable). He pairs softness (yielding to pain by choice) with self-indulgence, and endurance with continence. Continence is superior to endurance because conquering is greater than merely resisting.
distilled: Self-indulgence is incurable because it involves deliberate, unregretted choice of excess; continence surpasses mere endurance as winning surpasses not losing.
notes: The claim that the self-indulgent person is incurable because he cannot repent is one of Aristotle's strongest moral psychological claims, connecting moral improvement to the capacity for regret. -- The distinction between 'resisting' (anterchesthai) and 'conquering' (nikān) -- endurance versus continence -- is characteristically Aristotelian in its precision. -- The image of trailing one's cloak to avoid the effort of lifting it is a vivid picture of effeminacy (malakia) in the ancient Greek cultural context, where physical toughness was highly valued. -- This passage resolves parts of puzzles (2) and (3) from chapter 2 about the relationships among temperance, continence, self-indulgence, and incontinence.

refashioned:
The situation is similar regarding continence and incontinence. If someone is defeated by violent and excessive pleasures or pains, this is not surprising. Indeed, we are ready to pardon such a person if he has put up a fight -- as Philoctetes does when bitten by the serpent in Theodectes' play, or Cercyon in Carcinus' Alope, and as people who try to suppress their laughter burst out in a guffaw, as happened to Xenophantus.

But it is surprising and blameworthy if someone is defeated by and cannot resist pleasures or pains that most people can withstand -- unless the cause is heredity or disease, like the hereditary softness of the Scythian kings, or the natural difference in resilience between women and men.
elegant: Being overcome by overwhelming pleasure or pain is pardonable -- we excuse those who resist but are overpowered, as Philoctetes was by the serpent's bite, or as people who burst out laughing despite trying not to. But being defeated by what most people easily withstand is blameworthy, unless heredity or disease explains it.
summary: We pardon those overwhelmed by extreme pleasures or pains, especially when they have resisted. But someone who cannot withstand ordinary pleasures or pains that most people manage is rightly blamed -- unless the cause is hereditary or pathological, like the reported softness of the Scythian kings.
distilled: We pardon those overcome by extraordinary pressures but blame those who succumb to what most people easily resist, unless heredity or disease explains their weakness.
notes: Theodectes of Phaselis (c. 375-334 BCE) was a rhetorician and tragedian, a student of both Isocrates and Aristotle. His Philoctetes depicted the hero's agony from the wound inflicted by a sacred serpent on the island of Lemnos. -- Carcinus was a 4th-century BCE Athenian tragedian; his Alope dramatized the myth of Cercyon (king of Eleusis) and his daughter Alope. -- Xenophantus is otherwise unknown; the anecdote about suppressed laughter serves as a homely illustration of being overcome by irresistible impulse. -- The Scythians were nomadic peoples of the Eurasian steppe; Greek sources often commented on their customs. Herodotus (Histories 1.105, 4.67) mentions the 'female disease' among Scythian kings. -- The remark about female softness reflects conventional Greek gender assumptions.

refashioned:
The lover of amusement is also thought to be self-indulgent, but is really soft. For amusement is a form of relaxation -- a rest from work -- and the lover of amusement is simply someone who goes to excess in this.

Incontinence takes two forms: impetuosity and weakness. Some people deliberate but then fail, because of emotion, to stand by the conclusions of their deliberation. Others are led by emotion precisely because they have not deliberated at all. Some people -- just as those who tickle others first are not themselves tickled -- if they have first perceived and anticipated what is coming and have first roused themselves and their capacity for reasoning, are not defeated by their emotion, whether pleasant or painful. It is keen and excitable people who suffer especially from the impetuous form of incontinence. The keen act too quickly and the excitable are too passionate to wait for the argument; both are apt to follow their imagination instead.
elegant:
The amusement-lover is soft, not self-indulgent -- amusement is rest taken to excess.

Incontinence has two forms: impetuosity (acting before deliberating) and weakness (deliberating but failing to follow through). Those who anticipate temptation and prepare their reasoning in advance can resist emotion. Keen and excitable people are most prone to impetuous incontinence -- the keen because of their quickness, the excitable because of the violence of their passions.
summary: The lover of amusement is soft rather than self-indulgent. Incontinence takes two forms: impetuosity (acting on emotion before deliberating) and weakness (deliberating correctly but failing to follow through). Keen and excitable people are most prone to the impetuous variety because they act on imagination before reason can intervene.
distilled: Incontinence divides into impetuosity, where emotion acts before reason engages, and weakness, where emotion overrides reason's conclusions after deliberation.
notes: The distinction between impetuous (propeteia) and weak (astheneia) incontinence is one of Aristotle's most important contributions to the taxonomy of moral failure. -- The tickling analogy illustrates the advantage of anticipation: just as one cannot tickle oneself because the sensation is anticipated, anticipated temptation is easier to resist. -- This connects to Aristotle's general emphasis on habituation and preparation in moral development (Book II). -- The observation that amusement-lovers are soft rather than self-indulgent refines the categories established in 7.28-7.29.

refashioned:
The self-indulgent person, as noted, is not inclined to feel regret, since he stands by his deliberate choice. But the incontinent person is likely to feel regret. This is why the situation is not as the puzzle suggested: in fact, the self-indulgent person is incurable while the incontinent person is curable.
