For vice is like a chronic disease such as dropsy or tuberculosis, while incontinence is like epilepsy -- the former is a permanent badness, the latter an intermittent one.

More broadly, incontinence and vice differ in kind. Vice is unconscious of itself; incontinence is not. Among incontinent people themselves, those who become temporarily beside themselves are better than those who possess the rational principle but fail to abide by it, since the latter are defeated by a weaker passion and do not act without prior deliberation as the impetuous do. The incontinent person of the weak type is like people who get drunk quickly and on little wine -- that is, on less than it takes for most people.
elegant:
The self-indulgent person feels no regret and so is incurable; the incontinent person feels regret and so can be cured. Vice is like chronic disease (permanent); incontinence is like epilepsy (intermittent). Vice does not recognize itself; incontinence does.

Among the incontinent, the impetuous are better than the weak: the impetuous act without deliberation, while the weak deliberate correctly but are defeated by even mild passion -- like people who get drunk on little wine.
summary: Self-indulgence is incurable because it involves unregretted choice, while incontinence is curable because the incontinent person retains awareness that his actions are wrong. Vice and incontinence differ in kind: vice is permanent and self-unaware, incontinence is intermittent and self-conscious. Among the incontinent, the impetuous are better than the weak.
distilled: Vice is incurable because it is unaware of itself; incontinence is curable because the incontinent person knows he is acting wrongly and can feel regret.
notes: This passage resolves puzzle (5) from 7.9, which seemed to suggest the self-indulgent person was more curable. Aristotle reverses this: the incontinent person is more curable precisely because his principles remain intact. -- The disease metaphors (dropsy/consumption vs. epilepsy) were drawn from Hippocratic medicine: dropsy (edema) and consumption (phthisis/tuberculosis) were considered chronic and progressive, while epilepsy was episodic. -- The comparison of weak incontinence to getting drunk on little wine is a characteristically vivid Aristotelian analogy. -- The claim that vice is 'unconscious of itself' anticipates modern discussions of self-deception in moral philosophy.

refashioned: Evidently, then, incontinence is not vice -- though perhaps it is so in a qualified sense. For incontinence is contrary to one's deliberate choice, while vice is in accordance with it. Nevertheless, they are similar in the actions they produce. As Demodocus said about the Milesians: 'The Milesians are not without sense, but they do the things that senseless people do.' So too, incontinent people are not criminal, but they will do criminal acts.
elegant: Incontinence is not vice, since incontinence opposes one's own choice while vice accords with it. Yet they produce similar actions. As Demodocus said of the Milesians: 'They are not without sense, but they do what senseless people do.' Incontinent people are not criminals, but they commit criminal acts.
summary: Incontinence differs from vice in kind: vice acts in accordance with one's corrupted choice, while incontinence acts against one's correct choice. However, both produce the same bad actions, just as the Milesians were not senseless but did senseless things.
distilled: Incontinence is not vice because it opposes one's own choice rather than expressing it, even though the resulting actions may look identical.
notes: Demodocus was a poet (possibly 6th or 5th century BCE) known for satirical epigrams about various Greek cities. Miletus was a prosperous Ionian city on the coast of Asia Minor, and the butt of this particular joke. -- The distinction between incontinence and vice based on the role of choice (prohairesis) is fundamental to Aristotle's moral psychology and resolves a core puzzle of Book VII. -- This passage has been influential in philosophy of action: it shows that the moral character of an act depends not just on what is done but on the agent's relationship to the act.

refashioned:
Since the incontinent person is inclined to pursue bodily pleasures that are excessive and contrary to right reason -- but not from settled conviction -- while the self-indulgent person is convinced he should pursue them because that is the kind of person he is, it is actually the former who is more easily persuaded to change his mind, not the latter.

For virtue and vice respectively preserve and destroy the first principle of action. In action, the final cause serves as the first principle, just as hypotheses serve as first principles in mathematics. Neither in mathematics nor in ethics is it argument that teaches the first principles; rather, it is virtue -- whether natural or developed through habituation -- that teaches right opinion about the first principle. The person who has this right opinion about the end is temperate; the person whose first principles are corrupted is self-indulgent.
elegant: The incontinent person pursues excess without conviction and so can be persuaded to change; the self-indulgent person acts from conviction and cannot. Virtue preserves the first principle of action (the correct end to pursue), while vice destroys it. Just as mathematical reasoning cannot establish its own axioms, ethical reasoning cannot establish its own starting points -- virtue, natural or habitual, is what teaches correct judgment about the ultimate end.
summary: The incontinent person can be reformed because his fundamental moral principles are intact; the self-indulgent person cannot because vice has corrupted his first principles about what is good. Virtue teaches correct judgment about the ultimate end of action, just as mathematical axioms must be accepted before reasoning can proceed.
distilled: The first principles of moral action -- our fundamental sense of what is good -- are preserved by virtue and destroyed by vice, and no argument alone can restore what vice has corrupted.
notes: The analogy between ethical first principles and mathematical hypotheses (axioms) is one of Aristotle's most important methodological claims, connecting his Ethics to his logical works (Posterior Analytics I.2-3). -- The idea that virtue 'teaches' right opinion about the first principle means that moral perception -- seeing what is truly good -- depends on having a well-formed character, not on theoretical reasoning alone. -- This connects to Book VI.12-13's argument that practical wisdom requires moral virtue. -- The passage has been influential in virtue epistemology, which holds that intellectual virtues are necessary for proper knowledge.

refashioned:
But there is a kind of person who is carried away by passion contrary to right reason -- someone whom passion masters so that he does not act according to right reason, yet does not master to the extent of making him believe that he ought to pursue such pleasures without restraint. This is the incontinent person, who is better than the self-indulgent and not bad without qualification, because the best thing in him -- his first principle, his sense of what is truly good -- is preserved.

Opposite to him stands another kind of person: one who abides by his convictions and is not swept away by passion. It is clear from these considerations that the latter state is good and the former bad.
elegant: The incontinent person is mastered by passion in action but not in conviction -- he never comes to believe that unbounded pleasure-seeking is right. His first principle remains intact, making him better than the self-indulgent and not wholly bad. His opposite, the continent person who holds firm against passion, is in a genuinely good state.
summary: The incontinent person is not wholly bad because passion overrides his actions but not his fundamental beliefs about what is good. The continent person, who resists passion and holds to his convictions, is in a genuinely good state. What distinguishes them is whether the first principle of moral judgment survives intact.
distilled: The incontinent person retains correct moral principles even while acting against them, making him better than the self-indulgent person whose principles themselves are corrupted.
notes: The preservation of the 'first principle' (archē) in the incontinent person is the key to Aristotle's claim that incontinence is better than vice. -- This resolves puzzle (5) from 7.9 definitively: despite appearances, the incontinent person is more curable and less bad than the self-indulgent person. -- The passage establishes a clear moral hierarchy: temperance (best) > continence > incontinence > self-indulgence (worst). -- This analysis influenced Thomas Aquinas's treatment of weakness of will in the Summa Theologiae (I-II, q.77).

refashioned:
Is the continent person one who abides by any and every principle and choice, or specifically one who abides by the right choice? And is the incontinent person one who abandons any and every choice and principle, or specifically one who abandons the principle that is correct and the choice that is right? This was how we framed the puzzle earlier.

Or is it that incidentally the continent person abides by any and every opinion, but essentially he abides by the true principle and the right choice? When someone chooses or pursues one thing for the sake of another, what he essentially pursues and chooses is the latter, while the former is only incidental. And when we speak without qualification, we mean what is essential.

Therefore, in one sense the continent person abides by, and the incontinent person abandons, any and every opinion. But speaking without qualification, it is the true opinion that matters.
elegant: The continent person essentially abides by the right choice and true principle; the incontinent person essentially abandons them. When we call someone continent or incontinent without qualification, we mean their relationship to the correct rule, not to just any opinion they happen to hold.
summary: Aristotle resolves the puzzle about whether continence means standing by any belief or specifically the right one. Properly speaking, continence is about adhering to the true and right principle; incontinence is about abandoning it. Any other application is incidental.
distilled: Continence in the proper sense means abiding by the correct principle and right choice, not stubbornly clinging to any opinion whatsoever.
notes: This resolves puzzle (3) from 7.7 about the possibility of good incontinence and bad continence. -- The distinction between 'per se' (kath' hauto) and 'incidentally' (kata symbebēkos) is one of Aristotle's fundamental logical and metaphysical distinctions, appearing throughout the Organon, Physics, and Metaphysics. -- The resolution shows that while incidentally a continent person might cling to any belief, essentially (and in the unqualified sense) continence pertains only to right beliefs. -- This also resolves the Neoptolemus puzzle from 7.7: his abandonment of a false belief was not true incontinence.

refashioned:
There are people who tend to stick by their opinions and are called strong-headed or obstinate -- those who are hard to persuade in the first place and not easily moved to change. These people resemble the continent person in some ways, just as the prodigal resembles the generous person and the reckless person resembles the courageous one. But they differ in many respects.

The continent person refuses to yield to passion and appetite, and on other occasions will be easy enough to persuade by argument. The obstinate person, by contrast, refuses to yield to argument, while readily forming appetites and often being led by pleasures. The types of strong-headed people are the opinionated, the ignorant, and the boorish. The opinionated are motivated by pleasure and pain: they delight in the sense of victory when they are not persuaded to change, and feel pain when their positions are overturned -- like decrees that are nullified. In this way, they are actually more like the incontinent than the continent person.
elegant: The obstinate resemble the continent in clinging to their positions, but the resemblance is superficial. The continent person resists passion and appetite, remaining open to argument. The obstinate resist argument itself, driven by pleasure in being right and pain at being overruled. The opinionated, the ignorant, and the boorish are the main types of obstinate people -- and they are closer to the incontinent than the continent, since they are ultimately governed by pleasure and pain.
summary: Strong-headed or obstinate people resemble the continent in standing firm, but differ fundamentally: the continent person resists passion while remaining open to argument, whereas the obstinate resist argument itself, driven by the pleasure of seeming right and the pain of being refuted. This makes them closer to the incontinent than to the continent.
distilled: Obstinacy resembles continence superficially, but the obstinate person clings to opinions from pleasure and pain rather than reason, making him more like the incontinent person than the continent one.
notes: The comparison of the obstinate (ischyrognomos) to the continent parallels Aristotle's broader method of distinguishing genuine virtues from their look-alikes (e.g., true courage vs. recklessness in Book III). -- The analogy to decrees being overturned may refer to the Athenian practice of annulling laws (graphē paranomōn) -- a real source of political frustration. -- This analysis enriches Aristotle's account by showing that the mere behavior of standing firm does not constitute continence; the motivation matters.

refashioned:
There are some who fail to abide by their resolutions but not as a result of incontinence -- for example, Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes. He did not stand fast, and his abandonment was indeed for the sake of pleasure -- but a noble pleasure, for telling the truth was noble to him, and he had been persuaded by Odysseus to tell a lie.

Not everyone who does something for the sake of pleasure is self-indulgent or bad or incontinent. Only the person who does it for a disgraceful pleasure deserves those labels.
elegant: Neoptolemus in Sophocles' Philoctetes abandoned his resolution not from incontinence but for a noble pleasure -- the pleasure of truthfulness over the lie Odysseus had urged. Not all action for pleasure's sake is blameworthy; only action for disgraceful pleasure.
summary: Abandoning a resolution is not always incontinence. Neoptolemus abandoned Odysseus' plan of deception because truth-telling was noble and pleasant to him. Acting for the sake of pleasure is only blameworthy when the pleasure is disgraceful.
distilled: Abandoning a conviction for the sake of a noble pleasure, as Neoptolemus abandoned a lie for the pleasure of truthfulness, is not incontinence but moral integrity.
notes: This resolves puzzle (3) from 7.7, which used Neoptolemus as an apparent case of praiseworthy incontinence. Aristotle's solution: it was not incontinence at all, because the pleasure motivating his change was noble (truth) rather than base. -- Neoptolemus, son of Achilles, Odysseus, and Philoctetes are characters in Sophocles' Philoctetes (409 BCE). Odysseus engineers a scheme to steal Heracles' bow from the marooned Philoctetes, but Neoptolemus's natural honesty prevails. -- The distinction between noble and disgraceful pleasure is important for Aristotle's broader account of pleasure in chapters 11-14.

refashioned:
Since there is also a type of person who takes less delight than he should in bodily things and does not abide by the right principle for that reason, the continent person stands as the intermediate between this person and the incontinent. The incontinent person fails to abide by the principle because he delights too much in bodily pleasures; this other person fails because he delights in them too little. The continent person abides by the principle and does not deviate on either account.

Now if continence is good, both contrary states must be bad, as indeed they appear to be. But because the opposite extreme -- deficiency in bodily pleasure -- is seen in very few people and rarely, continence seems to be opposed only to incontinence, just as temperance seems opposed only to self-indulgence.
elegant: The continent person is the mean between two failures: the incontinent who delights too much in bodily pleasure, and the person who delights too little. Both contraries are bad, but because the deficient extreme is so rare, continence appears to have only one opposite -- incontinence -- just as temperance seems opposed only to self-indulgence.
summary: Continence is the intermediate state between incontinence (too much delight in bodily pleasure) and an unnamed deficiency (too little delight). Both extremes are bad, but the deficient extreme is so rare that continence appears to have only one opposite.
distilled: Continence is a mean between excessive and deficient delight in bodily pleasure, though the deficient extreme is so rare that it is usually overlooked.
notes: This applies the doctrine of the mean (Book II.6-8) to continence, though continence is itself not a full virtue but an intermediate condition between virtue and vice. -- The 'nameless' deficiency in bodily pleasure parallels similar unnamed extremes in Aristotle's virtue theory (e.g., the person deficient in anger, discussed in Book IV.5). -- The observation that one extreme is rare while the other is common reflects Aristotle's empirical approach to ethics and his awareness that the practical emphasis should be on the more common failing.

refashioned:
Since many terms are applied by analogy, we have come to speak of the 'continence' of the temperate person by analogy as well. For both the continent and the temperate person are such as to do nothing contrary to the right principle for the sake of bodily pleasures, but there is a crucial difference: the continent person has bad appetites while the temperate person does not. The temperate person is such as not to feel pleasure contrary to the right principle; the continent person feels such pleasure but is not led by it.

Similarly, the incontinent and the self-indulgent resemble each other -- they are different, but both pursue bodily pleasures. The difference is that the self-indulgent person also thinks he ought to pursue them, while the incontinent person does not think this.
elegant: We call the temperate person 'continent' by analogy, since both resist bodily pleasure -- but the temperate person has no bad appetites to resist, while the continent person has them and overcomes them. Similarly, both the incontinent and self-indulgent pursue bodily pleasure, but the self-indulgent person believes he should, while the incontinent person knows he should not.
summary: The temperate and the continent both avoid excess bodily pleasure, but the temperate person does so without internal conflict (having no bad desires), while the continent person succeeds despite having them. The self-indulgent and the incontinent both pursue excess, but the self-indulgent does so from conviction while the incontinent does so against his conviction.
distilled: Temperance and continence differ as effortless goodness differs from hard-won resistance; self-indulgence and incontinence differ as principled pursuit of pleasure differs from conflicted pursuit.
notes: This passage provides the clearest summary of the four-way distinction among temperance, continence, incontinence, and self-indulgence -- one of the most important classifications in Aristotle's moral psychology. -- The idea that temperance involves the absence of bad desires while continence involves overcoming them has generated extensive debate about whether Aristotle's ideal is closer to Kantian 'acting from duty against inclination' or to the effortless virtue emphasized by virtue ethicists. -- The analogical use of terms reflects Aristotle's broader theory of homonymy (Categories 1) and focal meaning (Metaphysics IV.2).

refashioned:
The same person cannot have practical wisdom and be incontinent. For it has been demonstrated that a person is practically wise only if he is also good in character. Furthermore, a person has practical wisdom not merely by knowing what is right but by being able to act on it -- and the incontinent person is precisely someone unable to act on what he knows.

However, nothing prevents a clever person from being incontinent. This is why people sometimes think that practically wise individuals are incontinent: because cleverness and practical wisdom differ in the way we described in our earlier discussions -- they are close in their reasoning but differ in their purpose.

Nor is the incontinent person like someone who knows and is actively contemplating a truth. Rather, he is like someone who is asleep or drunk. He acts willingly -- for he acts in a sense with knowledge of what he is doing and what end he is pursuing -- but he is not wicked, since his fundamental purpose is good. So he is, as it were, half-wicked. And he is not a criminal, for he does not act from malice aforethought. Of the two types of incontinent person, the weak type fails to abide by the conclusions of his deliberation, while the impetuous type does not deliberate at all.

The incontinent person is thus like a city that passes all the right decrees and has good laws but makes no use of them, as Anaxandrides jested:
elegant:
Practical wisdom and incontinence are incompatible: practical wisdom requires both knowing the good and acting on it, while the incontinent person cannot act on what he knows. Cleverness, however, is compatible with incontinence -- which explains why people confuse the two.

The incontinent person acts willingly and with a kind of knowledge, but his purpose remains good, making him 'half-wicked' -- not criminal, since he acts without malice. He resembles a city with excellent laws that are never enforced.
summary: Practical wisdom excludes incontinence because it requires not just knowing but acting rightly. Cleverness, which shares reasoning ability but not moral purpose, can coexist with incontinence. The incontinent person is 'half-wicked' -- his purpose is good but his actions are bad, like a city with good laws it never enforces.
distilled: The incontinent person is like a city with good laws but no enforcement -- his principles are sound but his will to act on them fails.
notes: The distinction between cleverness (deinotes) and practical wisdom (phronesis) was established in Book VI.12: cleverness is the ability to find means to any end, while practical wisdom directs this ability toward good ends. -- Anaxandrides was a 4th-century BCE Athenian comic playwright. The quotation continues in the next entry (7.42). -- The image of the city with unenforced laws is one of Aristotle's most memorable political analogies and reflects his constant interplay between ethical and political analysis. -- The 'half-wicked' characterization is uniquely Aristotelian and captures the paradox of a person whose character is divided against itself. -- This resolves puzzle (4) from 7.4 about whether the practically wise person can be incontinent.

refashioned:
'The city willed it -- that city which cares nothing for its laws.'

But the wicked person is like a city that does enforce its laws, except that the laws it enforces are wicked ones.

Now incontinence and continence concern what exceeds the condition characteristic of most people. The continent person abides by his resolutions more firmly than most people can manage, while the incontinent person does so less firmly than most.
elegant: The wicked person is like a city that enforces wicked laws -- the system works, but toward bad ends. Continence and incontinence mark the extremes beyond ordinary human capacity: the continent person holds firm more than most can, the incontinent person less.
summary: Completing the city analogy: the incontinent person has good laws (principles) but does not enforce them, while the wicked person enforces bad laws (corrupted principles). Continence and incontinence are defined by exceeding the normal range of human self-control in either direction.
distilled: The incontinent person fails to enforce good principles, while the wicked person efficiently enforces bad ones -- and both continence and incontinence exceed the normal range of human resolve.
notes: The quotation is from a lost play by Anaxandrides, an Athenian comic poet of the 4th century BCE. -- The city-laws analogy neatly captures the structural difference between incontinence (good principles, poor execution) and vice (corrupted principles, consistent execution). -- The claim that continence and incontinence exceed the normal human range places most people in an intermediate state -- neither fully continent nor fully incontinent -- consistent with Aristotle's general view that most people are morally mediocre (Book VII.7/7.28).

refashioned: Among the forms of incontinence, that of excitable or impetuous people is more curable than that of those who deliberate but fail to abide by their decisions. And those who are incontinent through acquired habit are more curable than those in whom incontinence is innate, since it is easier to change a habit than to change one's nature. Even habit is difficult to change precisely because it is like nature, as Evenus says:
elegant: Impetuous incontinence is more curable than weak incontinence, and habitual incontinence is more curable than innate. Habit is hard to change because it resembles nature -- but nature is harder still.
summary: Impetuous incontinence (acting before deliberation) is more curable than weak incontinence (deliberating but failing to follow through). Similarly, incontinence from habit is more curable than innate incontinence, though habit too is difficult to alter because it approximates a second nature.
distilled: The more deeply rooted the source of incontinence -- from momentary impulse to ingrained habit to inborn nature -- the harder it is to cure.
notes: Evenus of Paros (5th century BCE) was a poet and sophist mentioned several times in Plato's dialogues (Apology 20b, Phaedo 60d-61c). -- The idea that habit is a 'second nature' became proverbial in Western philosophy and was later developed extensively by Thomas Aquinas and the Scholastics. -- The ranking of curability (impetuous > habitual > innate) reflects Aristotle's practical orientation: ethics should help people improve, so understanding what is more or less changeable matters greatly. -- This also connects to the nature vs. nurture discussion in Book II about how virtues are acquired through habituation.

refashioned:
'I say that habit is nothing but long practice, friend, and this in the end becomes men's nature.'

We have now stated what continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness are, and how these states are related to each other.
elegant: As Evenus says: habit is long practice that becomes a second nature. This concludes the account of continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness, and their interrelations.
summary: Aristotle concludes the discussion of continence, incontinence, endurance, and softness with Evenus' verse that habit, through long practice, becomes second nature.
distilled: Habit, through long practice, becomes indistinguishable from nature -- completing the account of how self-control and its failures are structured.
notes: This verse from Evenus of Paros became one of the most quoted lines in ancient philosophy. -- The conclusion marks a major structural division in Book VII: chapters 1-10 (7.1-7.44) treat incontinence and continence; chapters 11-14 (7.45-7.60) turn to pleasure. -- Aristotle's emphasis on habit as quasi-nature connects to his foundational claim in Book II.1 that virtues are acquired through habituation, not by nature and not by teaching alone.

refashioned: The study of pleasure and pain falls within the province of the political philosopher, since he is the architect of the ultimate end with reference to which we call things good or bad without qualification. Moreover, this study is one of our necessary tasks: not only did we establish earlier that moral virtue and vice are concerned with pleasures and pains, but most people maintain that happiness involves pleasure. Indeed, the Greek word for the 'blessed' or supremely happy person is derived from a word meaning 'enjoyment.'
elegant: Pleasure and pain must be studied by the political philosopher, who determines the ultimate human good. Moral virtue and vice concern pleasures and pains, and most people identify happiness with pleasure -- the very word for 'blessed' derives from 'enjoyment.'
summary: Aristotle justifies turning to the topic of pleasure. The political philosopher must understand it because he determines the human good, moral virtue and vice are defined in terms of pleasure and pain, and common opinion closely links happiness with pleasure.
distilled: The study of pleasure is essential to ethics because moral virtue concerns pleasure and pain, and common opinion identifies happiness itself with enjoyment.
notes: The 'political philosopher' (politikos) is the ethicist in Aristotle's scheme, since ethics is a branch of political science (Book I.2). -- The etymological point connects makarios ('blessed') to chairein ('to enjoy/rejoice'), though modern linguists consider this etymology folk etymology. -- This begins the first of Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (the second is in Book X.1-5). Scholars debate whether these represent different periods of his thought or complementary analyses. -- The connection between virtue and pleasure/pain was established in Book II.3.

refashioned:
Now (1) some people think that no pleasure is a good, either in itself or incidentally, since the good and pleasure are not the same thing. (2) Others think that some pleasures are good but most are bad. (3) A third view holds that even if all pleasures are good, the best thing in the world still cannot be pleasure.

The reasons given for view (1) -- that pleasure is not a good at all -- are these: (a) Every pleasure is a perceptible process toward a natural state, and no process is the same in kind as its end -- for instance, the process of building is not the same kind of thing as a house. (b) The temperate person avoids pleasures. (c) The person of practical wisdom pursues what is free from pain, not what is pleasant. (d) Pleasures are a hindrance to thought, and the more one delights in them the greater the hindrance -- in sexual pleasure, for example, no one can think about anything else while absorbed in it. (e) There is no art of pleasure, yet every good is the product of some art. (f) Children and animals pursue pleasures.

The reasons for view (2) -- that not all pleasures are good -- are: (a) Some pleasures are actually base and worthy of reproach, and (b) some are harmful, since certain pleasant things are unhealthy.

The reason for view (3) -- that the best thing cannot be pleasure -- is that pleasure is not an end but a process.
elegant: Three anti-pleasure positions: (1) no pleasure is good, because pleasure is mere process, the temperate avoid it, it hinders thought, has no art, and is pursued by children and beasts; (2) not all pleasures are good, since some are base or harmful; (3) even if all pleasures are good, the supreme good cannot be pleasure, since pleasure is process, not end.
summary: Aristotle catalogues three views hostile to pleasure: that no pleasure is good at all (supported by six arguments), that most pleasures are bad (because some are base or harmful), and that the supreme good cannot be pleasure (because pleasure is a process rather than an end). These represent the positions he will systematically refute.
distilled: The main arguments against pleasure are that it is mere process rather than an end, that it hinders thought, that the wise avoid it, and that children and animals pursue it.
notes: View (1) is associated with Speusippus (Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy), who held that both pleasure and pain are evils, and the good is a neutral state. -- View (3) is broadly Platonic, drawing on arguments in the Philebus where Plato argues pleasure is an unlimited 'becoming' (genesis) rather than a 'being' (ousia). -- The argument that pleasure hinders thought (d) echoes a Platonic theme but Aristotle will argue this applies only to foreign (alien) pleasures, not to pleasures proper to an activity. -- The observation about sexual pleasure and thought anticipates Aristotle's more nuanced treatment in Book X.

refashioned:
These are more or less the arguments that have been offered. That it does not follow from them that pleasure is not a good, or even the chief good, is plain from the following considerations.

(A, a) First, since what is good can be so in two senses -- good without qualification and good for a particular person -- natural constitutions and states, and therefore the corresponding movements and processes, will admit of the same distinction. Among those pleasures thought to be bad, some are bad without qualification but not bad for a particular person (indeed they may be worthy of his choice), while others are not worthy of choice even for a particular person except at a particular time and for a brief period, though they are not bad without qualification. And still others are not really pleasures at all, but only appear to be -- namely, all those that involve pain and serve a curative purpose, such as the processes experienced by sick people.
elegant: The anti-pleasure arguments fail. 'Good' has two senses -- good simply and good for a particular person -- and pleasures divide accordingly. Some pleasures thought bad are not bad for a particular person; others are acceptable only temporarily; and some are not truly pleasures at all but curative processes involving pain that merely seem pleasant.
summary: Aristotle begins refuting the anti-pleasure arguments. Since 'good' means both 'good without qualification' and 'good for a particular person,' pleasures must be similarly divided. Some apparently bad pleasures are merely bad in general but appropriate for specific individuals or situations. Others are not truly pleasures but curative processes that only seem pleasant.
distilled: The case against pleasure collapses once we distinguish what is good simply from what is good for a particular person, and genuine pleasures from curative processes that merely resemble them.
notes: The distinction between 'good simply' (haplos agathon) and 'good for a particular person' (tini agathon) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics and appears throughout the Ethics, especially in the discussion of the good in Book I. -- The example of curative pleasures (sick persons enjoying remedies) draws on Hippocratic medical theory. -- This begins a systematic point-by-point refutation of the arguments catalogued in 7.46, following the order (A) through (G).

refashioned:
(b) Furthermore, since one kind of good is an activity and another is a state, the processes that restore us to our natural state are only incidentally pleasant. The activity at work in our appetites for these restorative things is actually the activity of whatever remains unimpaired in our state and nature. For there are pleasures that involve no pain or appetite at all -- the pleasures of contemplation, for instance -- where our nature is not deficient in any way.

That the restorative pleasures are only incidental is shown by this: people do not enjoy the same things when their nature is in its settled, healthy state as they do when it is being replenished. In the settled state they enjoy things that are pleasant without qualification; during replenishment they enjoy even their contraries -- sharp and bitter things that are not naturally pleasant or pleasant without qualification. The states these restorative pleasures produce are therefore not genuine pleasures in the natural or unqualified sense, for just as pleasant things differ, so do the pleasures that arise from them.
elegant: Restorative processes are only incidentally pleasant; the real pleasure comes from the activity of our unimpaired nature. Proof: the pleasures of contemplation involve no pain, appetite, or deficiency at all. And people in a healthy state enjoy different things than those being restored -- in recovery, even bitter things seem pleasant, though they are not pleasant by nature. True pleasure corresponds to the activity of a healthy, complete nature.
summary: Pleasures of restoration (eating when hungry, drinking when thirsty) are only incidentally pleasant -- the genuine pleasure comes from the activity of whatever remains healthy in our nature. The pleasures of contemplation, which involve no deficiency or appetite, prove that pleasure can exist without a restorative process. What we enjoy in health differs from what we enjoy during recovery.
distilled: Restorative pleasures are incidental; genuine pleasure is the activity of an unimpaired nature, as the pleasures of contemplation -- requiring no deficiency to exist -- demonstrate.
notes: The pleasures of contemplation (theōria) being cited as the purest example of non-restorative pleasure anticipates the argument of Book X.7-8 that contemplative activity is the highest happiness. -- The distinction between incidental and natural pleasure is crucial to Aristotle's defense of pleasure as a good: it allows him to acknowledge that some pleasures arise from deficiency while maintaining that the best pleasures do not. -- The observation that sick people enjoy bitter remedies comes from Greek medical practice, where sharp and bitter substances were used therapeutically.

refashioned:
(c) Again, it is not necessary that there be something better than pleasure, as some claim by arguing that the end is better than the process. For pleasures are not processes, nor do they all involve process -- they are activities and ends in themselves. They arise not when we are becoming something but when we are exercising some faculty. And not all pleasures have an end distinct from themselves; only the pleasures of those who are being led toward the completion of their nature have a separate end.

This is why it is wrong to call pleasure a 'perceptible process.' It should rather be called an activity of one's natural state, and instead of 'perceptible,' we should say 'unimpeded.' Some people think pleasure is a process only because they consider it to be good in the fullest sense, and they assume that activity is a kind of process -- but it is not.
elegant: Pleasures are not processes but activities and ends in themselves. They arise when we exercise a faculty, not when we are becoming something. Only restorative pleasures have an end beyond themselves. Pleasure should be defined not as 'perceptible process' but as 'unimpeded activity of the natural state.' The confusion arises from wrongly equating activity with process.
summary: Aristotle rejects the claim that pleasure is a mere process toward an end (and therefore inferior to that end). Pleasures are activities and ends in themselves, arising from the exercise of faculties in their natural state. The definition of pleasure should be 'unimpeded activity of the natural state,' not 'perceptible process.'
distilled: Pleasure is not a process toward something better but an unimpeded activity of our natural state -- an end in itself, not a means.
notes: This is one of Aristotle's most important philosophical claims about pleasure, directly contradicting Plato's characterization of pleasure as genesis (becoming/process) in the Philebus 53c-55a. -- The distinction between kinēsis (process/movement) and energeia (activity/actuality) is fundamental to Aristotle's metaphysics (Metaphysics IX.6) and his philosophy of action. An activity is complete at every moment, while a process is incomplete until it reaches its end. -- The definition of pleasure as 'unimpeded activity' anticipates the more refined account in Book X.4, where pleasure is said to 'complete' (teleioun) activity. -- This passage has been central to debates about whether Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (Books VII and X) are consistent.

refashioned:
(B) The view that pleasures are bad because some pleasant things are unhealthy is like saying that healthy things are bad because some healthy things are bad for making money. Both are bad in the respect mentioned, but they are not bad on that account -- indeed, thinking itself is sometimes injurious to health.

Neither practical wisdom nor any other state of being is impeded by the pleasure that arises from it. It is only foreign pleasures that interfere. The pleasures that arise from thinking and learning will actually make us think and learn all the more.
elegant: That some pleasant things are unhealthy no more proves pleasure bad than the fact that some healthy things hurt one's finances proves health bad. Thinking itself can harm health. The key distinction: pleasures proper to an activity enhance it, while foreign pleasures impede it. The pleasure of learning makes us learn more, not less.
summary: The argument that pleasure is bad because some pleasures are unhealthy commits a logical error -- it confuses incidental harm with essential badness. More importantly, pleasure proper to an activity enhances rather than hinders it: the pleasure of thinking promotes thinking. Only foreign pleasures (pleasures from unrelated activities) cause interference.
distilled: Pleasure proper to an activity enhances it; only foreign pleasures impede -- the joy of learning makes us learn more, not less.
notes: The distinction between proper (oikeia) and foreign (allotria) pleasures is one of Aristotle's most enduring contributions to the philosophy of pleasure and motivation. It reappears in Book X.5. -- This refutes argument (1d) from 7.46 about pleasure hindering thought: it is only sexual pleasure (a foreign pleasure relative to intellectual activity) that hinders thinking, not the pleasure of thinking itself. -- The analogy with health and money-making illustrates Aristotle's frequent use of the fallacy of accident (treating an incidental property as essential). -- The claim that the pleasure of learning enhances learning has modern empirical support in educational psychology and flow theory.

refashioned:
(C) That no pleasure is the product of any art arises naturally enough: there is no art that produces any other activity either, only arts that produce the corresponding capacity or faculty. That said, the arts of the perfumer and the cook are commonly considered arts of pleasure.

(D) The arguments that the temperate person avoids pleasure, that the practically wise person pursues a painless life, and that children and brutes pursue pleasure -- all of these are refuted by a single consideration. We have already pointed out in what sense pleasures are good without qualification and in what sense some are not. Now both animals and children pursue the latter kind -- and the practically wise person seeks tranquil freedom from precisely that kind -- namely, those pleasures that involve appetite and pain, that is, the bodily pleasures and their excesses, which define the self-indulgent person. This is why the temperate person avoids these particular pleasures. But even the temperate person has pleasures of his own.
elegant:
No art produces pleasure directly, but no art produces any activity directly -- only the capacity for it. The perfumer and cook are considered artists of pleasure nonetheless.

The temperate person avoids bodily pleasures involving appetite and pain, not all pleasure. Children and animals pursue exactly these lower pleasures. The practically wise person seeks freedom from these, not from pleasure as such. Even the temperate person has pleasures of his own.
summary: Aristotle dispatches two more objections. That no art produces pleasure is unsurprising, since no art produces any activity directly -- only the capacity for it. And the claim that the wise avoid pleasure while children and animals pursue it is refuted by distinguishing bodily pleasures (which involve appetite and pain) from higher pleasures. The temperate person avoids the former but enjoys the latter.
distilled: The temperate person avoids bodily pleasures that involve appetite and pain, not pleasure as such -- even he has pleasures proper to his virtuous activity.
notes: The mention of perfumers and cooks as 'artists of pleasure' reflects the Greek cultural context where these crafts were associated with luxury and sensuality. -- This refutes arguments (1b), (1c), (1e), and (1f) from 7.46 in a single stroke by distinguishing types of pleasure. -- The claim that the temperate person 'has pleasures of his own' is important: Aristotelian virtue is not asceticism but a proper ordering of pleasure. This connects to Book II.3 and Book X.1-5. -- The practically wise person's pursuit of freedom from bodily disturbance (aochlēsia) anticipates Epicurean ataraxia, though Aristotle's framework is quite different.

refashioned:
(E) Furthermore, it is agreed that pain is bad and to be avoided: some pain is bad without qualification, while other pain is bad because it impedes us in some way. Now the contrary of what is to be avoided, insofar as it is to be avoided and bad, is good. Pleasure, then, is necessarily a good.

Speusippus' response -- that pleasure is contrary both to pain and to the good, just as the greater is contrary both to the less and to the equal -- does not succeed. He would not want to say that pleasure is essentially just a species of evil.
elegant: Pain is agreed to be bad and to be avoided. The contrary of what is bad and to be avoided must be good. Therefore pleasure is necessarily a good. Speusippus' attempt to make pleasure contrary to both pain and the good -- as 'greater' is contrary to both 'less' and 'equal' -- fails, since he would not accept that pleasure is essentially a form of evil.
summary: Since pain is universally acknowledged to be bad, and pleasure is the contrary of pain, pleasure must be a good. Speusippus tried to avoid this conclusion by arguing that pleasure could be contrary to both pain and the good simultaneously, but this would make pleasure essentially evil -- which even he would not accept.
distilled: Since pain is acknowledged as bad and pleasure is its contrary, pleasure must be a good -- a conclusion that Speusippus' attempt to make pleasure neutral rather than good fails to block.
notes: Speusippus (c. 407-339 BCE) was Plato's nephew and his immediate successor as head of the Academy (347-339 BCE). He held that both pleasure and pain were evils, and the good was a neutral state -- a position Aristotle frequently criticizes. -- The analogy of 'greater' being contrary to both 'less' and 'equal' is a sophisticated logical point: Speusippus argued that just as a quantity can be opposed to two different things, pleasure can be opposed to both pain and the good. -- Aristotle's refutation turns on the principle that being contrary to something bad makes a thing good, not neutral or evil. -- This argument from contrariety was influential in later Stoic-Epicurean debates about the value of pleasure.

refashioned:
(F) And if certain pleasures are bad, this does not prevent the chief good from being some pleasure, just as the chief good may be some form of knowledge even though certain kinds of knowledge are bad.

Perhaps it is even necessary that if each disposition has unimpeded activities, and if happiness is the unimpeded activity of all our dispositions (or of some one of them), then this -- being the thing most worthy of choice -- is happiness. And this activity is pleasure. Thus the chief good would be some pleasure, even though most pleasures might be bad without qualification.

This is why all people think the happy life is pleasant and weave pleasure into their ideal of happiness -- and reasonably so, since no activity is complete when it is impeded, and happiness is something complete. This is why the happy person needs bodily goods and external goods (the goods of fortune): so that he may not be impeded in these ways.

Those who say that a good person is happy even on the rack or when falling into great misfortunes are talking nonsense, whether they mean to or not. And because we need fortune as well as other things, some people mistake good fortune for happiness itself. But it is not the same thing, since even good fortune when excessive becomes an impediment -- and perhaps should then no longer be called good fortune, since its proper limit is set by reference to happiness.
elegant:
That some pleasures are bad does not prevent the chief good from being a pleasure, any more than bad forms of knowledge prevent the chief good from being a form of knowledge. If happiness is the unimpeded activity of our best disposition, and this activity is pleasant, then the chief good is a kind of pleasure.

All people rightly weave pleasure into happiness, since no impeded activity is complete, and happiness must be complete. The happy person needs bodily and external goods to avoid impediment. Those who call a person happy on the rack are talking nonsense. Good fortune serves happiness but is not identical to it -- and in excess, even fortune becomes an impediment.
summary: Some pleasures being bad does not prevent the supreme good from being a type of pleasure. If happiness is the unimpeded activity of our highest capacity, and such activity is inherently pleasant, then the chief good is a kind of pleasure. Happiness requires bodily and external goods to remain unimpeded. Neither torture nor excessive fortune is compatible with happiness.
distilled: The chief good may well be a kind of pleasure, since happiness is unimpeded excellent activity, and such activity is inherently and necessarily pleasant.
notes: The argument that happiness requires external goods (bodily health, wealth, friends) is characteristic of Aristotle's rejection of the Stoic-like view that virtue alone suffices for happiness. This connects to Book I.8-10. -- The dismissal of the 'happy on the rack' position targets views associated with the Cynics and later the Stoics, who held that virtue alone guaranteed happiness regardless of external circumstances. -- The idea that excessive fortune is an impediment is a distinctively Aristotelian insight: too much wealth or beauty can distract from virtuous activity. -- The identification of pleasure with unimpeded activity here anticipates Book X.4-5's more refined account. -- This is arguably the strongest passage in Book VII for the view that Aristotle identifies the chief good with pleasure in some sense.

refashioned:
Indeed, the fact that all creatures -- both animals and humans -- pursue pleasure is an indication that pleasure is somehow the chief good:

'No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak...'

But since no single nature or condition is or is thought to be best for all, not all creatures pursue the same pleasure -- yet all pursue pleasure. And perhaps they are not actually pursuing the pleasure they think they pursue or would say they pursue, but the same underlying pleasure. For all things have by nature something divine in them.

Bodily pleasures, however, have appropriated the name 'pleasure' as their own, both because we most often steer our course toward them and because everyone shares in them. Since they alone are familiar, people assume there are no other kinds.
elegant:
That all creatures pursue pleasure suggests it is somehow the chief good -- 'No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak.' Not all pursue the same pleasure, since natures differ, yet all pursue pleasure. Perhaps all things, having something divine in them, unknowingly pursue the same deep pleasure.

Bodily pleasures have monopolized the name 'pleasure' because they are the most common and familiar, leading people to suppose no other pleasures exist.
summary: The universal pursuit of pleasure by all creatures suggests it is connected to the chief good. Though different beings pursue different pleasures, there may be a single underlying pleasure all are drawn toward, reflecting something divine in nature. Bodily pleasures have monopolized the concept of 'pleasure' because of their universality and familiarity, obscuring the existence of higher pleasures.
distilled: The universal pursuit of pleasure across all creatures points to something divine in nature, though bodily pleasures have monopolized the name and obscured the existence of higher forms.
notes: The quoted verse ('No voice is wholly lost that many peoples speak...') is from Hesiod's Works and Days (763-764), used to support the principle that widespread opinion contains some truth -- a principle central to Aristotle's dialectical method. -- The claim that 'all things have by nature something divine in them' echoes Aristotle's teleological metaphysics and connects to his argument in Metaphysics XII that all things are oriented toward the divine unmoved mover. -- The observation that bodily pleasures have 'appropriated the name' anticipates the modern philosophical distinction between lower and higher pleasures, later developed by John Stuart Mill in Utilitarianism (1863). -- This passage contains one of Aristotle's most expansive and almost mystical claims about the unity of pleasure across all living things.

refashioned:
It is also evident that if pleasure -- that is, the activity of our faculties -- is not a good, then the happy person will not live a pleasant life. For why would he need pleasure if it is not a good? The happy person might even live a painful life -- for pain would be neither evil nor good if pleasure is neither. So why should he avoid pain?

Furthermore, the life of the good person will not be pleasanter than anyone else's, if his activities are not more pleasant.
elegant: If pleasure is not a good, the happy person need not live pleasantly -- and might even live painfully, since pain would be no more an evil than pleasure is a good. But this is absurd. Moreover, the good person's life would be no pleasanter than anyone else's, which contradicts our intuitions about virtue and happiness.
summary: If pleasure were not a good, the happy person would have no reason to live pleasantly and might even live in pain without it being a problem. This absurd consequence, along with the implication that the virtuous life would be no more pleasant than any other, shows that pleasure must be a genuine good.
distilled: Denying that pleasure is a good leads to the absurdity that the happy person's life need not be pleasant and the virtuous life would be no better than any other.
notes: This is a reductio ad absurdum argument: if pleasure is not a good, several plainly false consequences follow. -- The claim that the good person's activities should be more pleasant connects to Book I.8, where Aristotle argues that virtuous activities are inherently pleasant to the person of good character. -- The argument also connects to Book X.7-8, where Aristotle argues that the contemplative life is the most pleasant as well as the most excellent. -- The passage implicitly targets Speusippus and others who tried to separate pleasure entirely from the good.

refashioned:
(G) Regarding bodily pleasures: those who maintain that some pleasures are very much worth choosing -- namely the noble ones -- but not the bodily pleasures that concern the self-indulgent person, must consider why the corresponding bodily pains are bad. For the contrary of something bad is good.

Are the necessary pleasures good merely in the sense that even what is not bad qualifies as good? Or are they good up to a certain point? Where you have states and processes that do not admit of excess, the corresponding pleasure cannot be excessive either. But where there can be too much of the state, there can be too much of the corresponding pleasure as well.

Now there can be too much of bodily goods. The bad person is bad by virtue of pursuing the excess, not by pursuing the necessary pleasures themselves -- for everyone enjoys good food, wine, and sexual intercourse in some fashion, though not everyone does so as they ought. The reverse holds for pain: the bad person does not merely avoid excessive pain; he avoids pain altogether. And this is peculiar to him, since the alternative to excessive pleasure is not pain -- except for the person who specifically pursues that excess.
elegant: If bodily pleasures are not good, why are the corresponding bodily pains bad? Necessary pleasures are good up to a point -- where excess in the underlying state is possible, excess in pleasure is possible too. Everyone enjoys food, wine, and sex to some degree; the bad person errs by pursuing the excess, not the necessity. Conversely, the bad person avoids all pain, not just excessive pain.
summary: Those who reject bodily pleasures as goods must explain why bodily pains are evils. Necessary pleasures are genuinely good up to a point. The fault of the self-indulgent lies not in enjoying food, wine, and sex (which everyone does) but in pursuing their excess. The bad person's distinctive error with pain is avoiding it altogether rather than just avoiding its excess.
distilled: Necessary bodily pleasures are good up to a point; the fault lies in pursuing their excess, not in enjoying them, and the corresponding pains confirm their goodness by being genuinely bad.
notes: This addresses the position (G) that noble pleasures are good but bodily ones are not. -- The argument from contraries (if the pain is bad, the corresponding pleasure must be good) extends the logic used in 7.52. -- The distinction between pursuing necessary pleasures (acceptable) and their excess (vicious) connects to Book III.11's account of temperance. -- The observation that everyone enjoys 'dainty foods, wines, and sexual intercourse' reflects Aristotle's realism about human nature and his refusal to endorse asceticism.

refashioned:
Since we should state not only the truth but also the cause of error -- for explaining why the false view appears true helps produce conviction in the true view -- we must explain why bodily pleasures appear to be the most choiceworthy.

(a) First, it is because they expel pain. When people experience great pain, they pursue excessive bodily pleasure as a remedy. These curative pleasures produce intense feeling -- which is precisely why they are sought -- because they stand out against the background of pain. (Indeed, pleasure is thought not to be good for two reasons, as already mentioned: first, some pleasures are activities belonging to a bad nature, whether congenital, as in brutes, or acquired through habit, as in bad people; second, other pleasures are meant to cure a defective nature, and it is better to be in a healthy state than to be getting into one -- but these curative pleasures arise during the process of being restored and are therefore only incidentally good.)

(b) Second, bodily pleasures are pursued for their intensity by people who cannot enjoy other pleasures. They even go out of their way to manufacture artificial thirsts for themselves. When these artificial desires are harmless, the practice is unobjectionable; when they are harmful, it is bad. For such people have nothing else to enjoy, and besides, a neutral state -- neither pleasure nor pain -- is actually painful to many people because of their nature.
elegant: We must explain why bodily pleasures seem most desirable. First, they expel pain: intense curative pleasures stand out against a background of suffering, making them seem supremely good -- though they are only incidentally pleasant. Second, people who cannot enjoy higher pleasures pursue bodily ones for their sheer intensity, even manufacturing artificial appetites. For some, a neutral state is itself painful, driving them toward any strong sensation.
summary: Aristotle explains why people mistakenly prize bodily pleasure above all else. First, bodily pleasures are curative -- they relieve pain intensely, which makes them seem supremely valuable though they are only incidentally good. Second, people who lack access to higher pleasures pursue bodily intensity as the only available option, sometimes even creating artificial appetites. For some natures, even a neutral state is painful.
distilled: Bodily pleasures seem supreme because they relieve pain with intense feeling and are the only pleasure available to those who cannot access higher forms of enjoyment.
notes: The principle that explaining the cause of error strengthens belief in the truth is an important methodological claim, connecting to Aristotle's dialectical practice in the Topics and his discussion of rhetorical persuasion in the Rhetoric. -- The 'manufactured thirsts' may refer to practices like eating salty or spicy foods to increase the pleasure of drinking -- a phenomenon recognized in Greek symposium culture. -- The observation that a neutral state is painful for some people anticipates modern psychological research on sensation-seeking personality types and boredom intolerance. -- The two reasons pleasure seems bad (bad nature, curative process) summarize Aristotle's analysis from 7.47-7.48.

refashioned:
For animal nature is always in a state of strain, as the students of natural science also attest, saying that even sight and hearing are painful -- we have simply become habituated to them, they maintain.

Similarly, young people, because of the growth occurring in them, are in a state resembling intoxication, and youth is pleasant precisely for this reason. On the other hand, people of excitable temperament always need relief: even their body is constantly in a state of agitation due to their natural constitution, and they are perpetually under the influence of intense desire. Pain is driven out both by the contrary pleasure and by any chance pleasure, so long as it is strong enough. And it is for these reasons that such people become self-indulgent and bad.
elegant: Animal nature is always under strain -- even basic sensation involves a kind of pain we have grown accustomed to. Youth resembles intoxication because of the body's constant growth. Excitable temperaments are in perpetual agitation, always craving relief through strong sensation. Pain is driven out by any sufficiently intense contrary pleasure -- which is why such people become self-indulgent.
summary: According to natural science, all animal sensation involves a degree of strain. Youth resembles intoxication because of bodily growth. People with excitable temperaments are in constant physical agitation and always crave intense relief through pleasure. This perpetual need for strong sensation explains how they become self-indulgent.
distilled: Excitable natures are in constant physical agitation and therefore perpetually crave intense pleasure as relief, which is how they develop into self-indulgent people.
notes: The claim that sight and hearing are painful comes from natural philosophers, possibly Anaxagoras or the Hippocratic tradition, who held that all sensation involves a degree of alteration (alloiōsis) that is inherently painful. -- The comparison of youth to intoxication reflects the Greek medical view that the growing body is in constant flux. -- The 'excitable nature' (melancholikos in some readings) may refer to the melancholic temperament in Hippocratic medicine, associated with black bile and restlessness. -- This passage provides a naturalistic, almost physiological explanation for self-indulgence, complementing the moral-psychological account of earlier chapters.

refashioned:
But the pleasures that do not involve pain admit of no excess. These belong to things that are naturally pleasant, not incidentally so.

By things 'incidentally pleasant' I mean those that act as cures -- the reason the process seems pleasant is that people are restored to health through the action of whatever part of them remains healthy. By things 'naturally pleasant' I mean those that stimulate the activity of a healthy nature as such.
elegant: Pleasures not involving pain cannot be excessive. These are naturally pleasant -- they stimulate the activity of a healthy nature. Incidentally pleasant things are merely curative: they seem pleasant because recovery occurs through the activity of what remains healthy.
summary: Pleasures that involve no pain or deficiency cannot be excessive and are naturally pleasant, stimulating the activity of a healthy nature. Curative pleasures are only incidentally pleasant -- they seem pleasant because the healthy part of one's nature is active during the healing process.
distilled: Pleasures arising from the activity of a healthy nature cannot be excessive; only curative pleasures -- which are incidentally pleasant -- admit of excess.
notes: This distinction between naturally pleasant (kata phusin) and incidentally pleasant (kata symbebēkos) is crucial to Aristotle's defense of pleasure. The pleasures of contemplation and virtuous activity are naturally pleasant and cannot be excessive, while bodily pleasures of restoration can be. -- The claim that natural pleasures admit no excess provides the philosophical basis for the view that the highest forms of pleasure (intellectual, contemplative) are immune to the problems associated with bodily pleasure. -- This anticipates Book X.4-5's refined account of pleasure as the completion of activity.

refashioned:
There is no single thing that is always pleasant, because our nature is not simple. There is another element within us as well, since we are perishable creatures. When one element in us does something, this is unnatural for the other element. When the two are evenly balanced, what is done seems neither painful nor pleasant. If any being's nature were truly simple, the same activity would always be most pleasant to it.

This is why God always enjoys a single, simple pleasure. For there is not only an activity of movement but also an activity of rest, and pleasure is found more in rest than in movement. But 'change in all things is sweet,' as the poet says, because of a kind of deficiency. For just as a changeable person is deficient in character, so a nature that needs change is deficient -- it is neither simple nor wholly good.

We have now discussed continence and incontinence, and pleasure and pain -- both what each is and in what sense some of them are good and others bad. It remains to speak of friendship.
elegant:
No single thing is always pleasant because human nature is composite: what satisfies one element in us conflicts with another, and only perfect balance produces neutrality. A simple nature would find the same activity always most pleasant. This is why God enjoys a single, unchanging pleasure -- for pleasure resides more in rest than in movement.

'Change in all things is sweet' only because of a deficiency: the nature that craves change, like the changeable person, is not simple or wholly good.

This completes the discussion of continence, incontinence, pleasure, and pain. Friendship comes next.
summary: No single pleasure is permanent for humans because our composite nature means that satisfying one element conflicts with another. God, having a simple nature, enjoys a single unchanging pleasure. The proverb that 'change is sweet' reflects a deficiency in our nature, not a truth about pleasure itself. Aristotle concludes the discussion of pleasure and transitions to friendship.
distilled: Our love of variety in pleasure reflects the imperfection of our composite nature; a perfectly simple being like God would enjoy a single, unchanging pleasure forever.
notes: The quotation 'change in all things is sweet' is from Euripides (Orestes 234). -- The theological point that God enjoys a single, simple pleasure connects to Metaphysics XII.7-9, where the divine thinking-of-thinking (noēsis noēseōs) is described as the highest actuality. -- The contrast between divine simplicity and human complexity is fundamental to Aristotle's cosmology and theology. -- The idea that pleasure in rest exceeds pleasure in motion echoes the priority of actuality (energeia) over potentiality (dynamis) in Aristotle's metaphysics. -- This passage concludes Book VII. The transition to friendship (Books VIII-IX) follows, comprising the longest single treatment of any topic in the Ethics. -- Aristotle's two treatments of pleasure (Book VII.11-14 and Book X.1-5) have generated extensive scholarly debate about their consistency. Book VII emphasizes pleasure as unimpeded activity; Book X describes pleasure as something that 'completes' activity like the bloom on youth.
